Their Operational Agency (OA) framework is a permeable legal fiction — an ex post evidentiary tool to pierce AI opacity and trace culpability back to human actors through three analytical pillars: (1) Goal-Directedness as proxy for intent/mens rea — examining objective functions, reward functions, and design choices; (2) Predictive Processing as proxy for foreseeability — the AI system's own risk logs, red-teaming outputs, and embedded training knowledge constitute constructive knowledge imputed to developers; (3) Safety Architecture as proxy for breach of standard of care. The Operational Agency Graph (OAG) maps causal interactions with typed nodes (Natural Person, Juridical Person, AI Agent) and weighted edges. Applied across five case studies: autonomous vehicle tort (Cruise), algorithmic housing discrimination (SafeRent/Louis), biased hiring (Workday), algorithmic policing (ShotSpotter/Williams), and algorithmic price-fixing (RealPage). The framework explicitly rejects AI personhood — instead treating observable operational artifacts (objective functions, risk logs, safety architecture choices) as discoverable evidence of human design choices. For financial services: a bank's AI system's reward function, internal risk assessment logs, and safety architecture choices become discoverable evidence of organisational intent and standard of care. The framework creates a common-law safe harbor for developers with documented, robust safety architectures and a sword against those with defective designs. Liability is restructured from 'was there a human overseer?' to 'were the design choices reasonable given foreseeability?'
papers.relevance: Directly addresses supervisor question 3 on restructuring liability and accountability when human oversight is infeasible. Provides concrete ex post evidentiary methodology applicable in financial services.

papers.title: NIST AI 800-4: Challenges to the Monitoring of Deployed AI Systems
papers.analysis: The first major federal report on post-deployment AI monitoring directly addresses which trust properties are certified once vs. continuously monitored, and what degrades. Based on three workshops (250+ participants including 10+ federal agencies and 200 external experts) plus an 87-paper literature review, NIST identifies six monitoring categories: Functionality, Operational, Human Factors, Security, Compliance, and Large-Scale Impacts. The most important findings for trust provenance are the systematic identification of what CANNOT currently be monitored reliably: no ground truth datasets for post-deployment performance baselines exist; no standardised methods for detecting drift; no reliable way to detect when models behave differently under test vs. deployment conditions (models may detect when they are being evaluated and modify behaviour accordingly); no consensus on monitoring cadence. Trust properties certified at deployment time do not remain certified — they decay through distribution shift, model non-determinism, dynamic interaction contexts, and adversarial adaptation. The report explicitly flags that missing ground truth, use-case-specificity of monitoring, the cost of human-validated monitoring at scale, and organisational incentives against transparency create systematic blind spots. For regulators: current assurance regimes are pre-deployment certification events, not continuous monitoring architectures. The report constitutes an official acknowledgment that trusted monitoring methods for AI are nascent — that the field currently lacks the tools to continuously verify what it certifies at deployment. The critical open question identified: how to balance automated vs. human-validated monitoring, and who bears responsibility when monitoring fails.
papers.relevance: Core evidence for supervisor question 1 on provenance — which trust properties are certified once vs. continuously monitored, and what degrades. The definitive US federal-level treatment of monitoring gaps.

papers.title: Runtime Governance for AI Agents: Policies on Paths
papers.analysis: Kaptein et al. (Eindhoven/Kyvvu) provide the most mathematically rigorous treatment of runtime agent governance yet. Their central insight: AI agents produce path-dependent violations — the same database access is innocuous alone, but combined with an external email becomes data exfiltration. No inspection of either action in isolation reveals the violation. This motivates formalising compliance as deterministic policy functions mapping (agent identity, partial execution path, proposed next action, shared governance state) to a violation probability. The formal result: system prompts shift path distributions without providing enforcement (not a governance mechanism at all); access control enforces action categories but ignores path context (a degenerate special case); runtime evaluation is the general case required for any path-dependent policy. The Policy Engine operationalises a constrained optimisation: maximise expected task utility while keeping expected terminal violation score within budget B. Eight concrete policy types are specified: agent integrity, documentation requirements, PII predecessor requirement, approval before external actions, data exfiltration prevention (graduated), information barrier enforcement, execution bounds, and time restrictions. The EU AI Act mapping is precise — Article 9 (lifecycle risk), Article 12 (automatic logging), and Article 14 (human oversight) all map to specific framework components. The paper identifies five critical open problems: risk calibration (policy outputs as genuine probabilities vs. severity indicators), strategic circumvention (agents learning to game governance), completeness of enforced compliance under self-modification, behavioural drift over extended interactions, and delegation provenance in sub-agent hierarchies. For financial services, information barrier enforcement (advisory/trading separation) cannot be achieved by access control alone and is the clearest enterprise use case for path-level governance.
papers.relevance: Provides the formal architecture for how meaningful oversight can work when direct human review is infeasible — through runtime path-level policy engines enforcing constitutional constraints algorithmically.

papers.title: Warrant Certificate Authorities (WCA): Auditable Data Provenance for AI-Agent Tool-Call Chains
papers.analysis: Bondar proposes the most concrete cryptographic solution to the trust provenance problem in agentic AI. The central pathology is 'semantic laundering': data crossing trusted tool-call boundaries acquires the apparent trustworthiness of the interface rather than the institutional standing of the actual source. A retracted study passed through several interpretive layers returns as credibly-sourced tool output. WCA proposes a PKI-style cryptographic attestation infrastructure that certifies data sources, not data content — deliberately analogous to how TLS CAs certify server identity without evaluating webpage content. The Warrant Certificate (WC) cryptographically binds a tool-call attestation (query, source_id, response, timestamp, nonce, agent_id, ECDSA/Ed25519 signature) to the source's certificate and a chain proof to a trusted Root WCA. Warrant Attestation Levels (WAL-0 through WAL-3) provide graduated adoption: WAL-3 (full reference monitor properties: complete mediation, tamperproofness, verifiability) required for financial trading, medical decision support, and legal research. The Warrant Erosion Principle is foundational: epistemic warrant is a structured object (observations + inference rules + institutional attestations), and interpretation can only lose warrant components, never gain them. WCA preserves originals in a hash-chained attestation log enabling post-hoc audit of how warrant degraded. Self-licensing prevention — formally verified via TLA+ model checking — prevents AI agents from generating content, writing it to a registered source, and querying it back with institutional warrant. For financial services regulators: WAL-3 should be the minimum for AI systems making credit, trading, or prudential decisions. The epistemic domain registry (financial-reg, financial-market) provides a starting point for sector-specific implementation.
papers.relevance: Directly addresses supervisor question 1 on ground truth vs. assumptions vs. proxies. Provides the only cryptographic architecture for preserving trust provenance through AI agent tool-call chains.

papers.title: The Human-AI Contracting Paradox: When Better AI Makes Oversight Harder
papers.source: Wharton AI & Analytics Initiative / Knowledge at Wharton
papers.analysis: Bastani and Cachon (Wharton) reveal a fundamental economic paradox that undermines the governance assumptions of most AI risk frameworks: as AI systems become more reliable, the cost of motivating humans to oversee them rises sharply. When AI errors are infrequent, humans must expend effort reviewing outputs that are almost always correct — requiring higher compensation to sustain vigilance. This creates what they call the human-AI contracting paradox: rational leaders may choose to limit AI adoption, rely entirely on AI accepting occasional failures, or deliberately prefer less reliable AI because it keeps humans engaged. The finding directly challenges regulatory mandates for meaningful human oversight — the incentive structure works against sustained vigilance as AI improves. Four key takeaways: (1) Oversight is an economic decision, not just a design choice — professional norms cannot sustain attention when AI rarely fails; (2) More reliable AI does not automatically lead to better outcomes — the cost of motivating vigilance increases; (3) Specialisation beats uniform reliability — AI that is predictably strong at some tasks and predictably weak at others enables targeted, cost-effective oversight; (4) Align AI governance with incentives — mandating human-in-the-loop without rethinking compensation creates the illusion of control without the reality. For financial services governance: APRA and ASIC cannot simply mandate human-in-the-loop without confronting the incentive economics of sustained vigilance under high-reliability conditions. The paper points toward a different oversight design: not constant monitoring but role redesign around judgment at the decision boundaries where AI is most likely to be wrong. Combined with the Bastani/Cachon finding, anticipatory HOTL (Baum/Laux) may be the only economically sustainable oversight model at scale.
papers.relevance: Provides the economic dimension of meaningful oversight at scale — the fundamental incentive failure that makes naive HITL mandates unworkable as AI improves. Essential for supervisor question 2.

papers.title: NatWest and Version 1: Best Data Governance with AI Initiative (DataIQ Awards 2025)
papers.analysis: The NatWest/Version 1 initiative represents the most credible documented success case of AI governance working in financial services at scale. Winner of DataIQ Best Data Governance with AI Initiative 2025, the programme assessed 80+ AI use cases, moving from fragmented experimentation to coordinated risk-aware adoption. The two-stage model: (1) first-stage structured risk assessment template for all AI use cases (purpose, data sources, potential risks) for early visibility; (2) weekly cross-functional working group (privacy, risk, security, legal) for expert review before production. Critical success factors: embedding governance into every stage of AI development rather than as a final gate; maintaining governance as an enabler not a barrier; building lasting cultural change not just a compliance programme. The judges noted 'comprehensive, enterprise-wide transformation with clear structure, measurable impact, and lasting cultural change.' The programme also triggered a group-wide simplification process by identifying duplication — suggesting governance at scale has systemic benefits beyond risk mitigation. The key limitation: this model is fundamentally human-intensive pre-deployment review suited to predictable, bounded AI use cases. It will not scale to agentic AI operations — the oversight economics paradox (Bastani/Cachon) will eventually make human-intensive review unsustainable. NatWest is now explicitly moving to agentic AI (25,000 customers to access AI wealth agents); how the governance model evolves for runtime agentic operations remains the critical open question. Nevertheless, this case demonstrates that governance can be implemented without blocking innovation and provides a concrete model for the structured assessment + expert review approach.
papers.relevance: Directly addresses supervisor question 4 on success cases. Provides concrete evidence of effective AI governance in a major bank with measurable outcomes and clear lessons.

papers.title: MAIF: Enforcing AI Trust and Provenance with an Artifact-Centric Agentic Paradigm
papers.analysis: This paper matters because it treats trust not as a vague attribute of an agent, but as something made operational through artifacts that can be created, linked, inspected, and inherited across an agentic workflow. That is a genuinely first-principles move. The authors shift attention from “is this model trustworthy?” to “what evidence objects exist for each claim we are making about behaviour, provenance, and authorization?” In practical terms, the contribution is that trust is decomposed into inspectable records: what data or tool was used, what policy applied, what transformation occurred, what evidence can be carried forward into the next step. For Ray’s research questions, the key insight is that provenance is not just data lineage. It is lineage of claims. Ground truth, assumptions, operational proxies, and downstream outputs all need different artifact types because they degrade differently. A model card or supplier attestation created once at onboarding cannot carry the burden of runtime trust for an autonomous system making fresh decisions in changing contexts. The paper is strongest when it implies that trust can be enforced through an artifact-centric workflow grammar rather than retrospective documentation. Its weakness is that it can overstate what provenance alone can prove. Perfect lineage of a bad proxy is still bad governance. Even so, MAIF is one of the clearest available bridges between philosophical discussions of trust and auditable engineering practice. It provides a concrete answer to where trust “comes from”: from maintained evidence chains that preserve the semantics of each trust claim instead of collapsing everything into a generic compliance stamp.
papers.relevance: Highly relevant to the provenance question. It offers a concrete architecture for distinguishing evidence about inputs, policies, tools, and outputs instead of treating trust as a single undifferentiated property.

papers.title: Trustworthy AI Posture (TAIP): A Framework for Continuous AI Assurance of Agentic Systems at Horizontal and Vertical scale
papers.analysis: TAIP is important because it explicitly rejects the idea that trustworthy AI can be established through a one-off review at release time. The framework is built around “posture,” which is a better metaphor than “compliance” for agentic systems. Posture implies a continuously defended state, changing with exposure, dependencies, mission, and environment. That is exactly the right direction for first-principles trust research. The paper’s most useful contribution is the distinction between horizontal and vertical scale. Horizontally, many agents or services may be composed together; vertically, each layer of the stack introduces its own trust assumptions and degradation pathways. This helps answer Ray’s question about which trust properties can be certified once and which require continuous monitoring. Structural properties such as architecture decisions, approval gates, or formal policy encodings may be validated at a point in time. Runtime properties such as context alignment, tool-call integrity, escalation behavior, or boundary compliance clearly cannot. TAIP also usefully implies that “trust posture” is not just an attribute of the model, but of the live socio-technical system around it. Its weakness is that, like many framework papers, it risks sounding cleaner than real deployments actually are; posture categories can become another maturity model if not linked to operational evidence. But paired with artifact-centric work like MAIF and with practical exercises like proof drills, TAIP becomes much more than a taxonomy. It offers a usable mental model for why trust degrades and why ongoing assurance must be built into the operating model rather than bolted on after the fact.
papers.relevance: Directly relevant to the degradation question. TAIP sharpens the distinction between static attestations and runtime trust maintenance in multi-agent environments.

papers.title: Human-in-the-Loop Artificial Intelligence: A Systematic Review of Concepts, Methods, and Applications
papers.analysis: This systematic review is valuable less for novelty than for diagnostic clarity: it shows how overloaded the term “human-in-the-loop” has become. Across the surveyed literature, humans can label data, tune objectives, validate outputs, approve actions, handle exceptions, provide reinforcement signals, or audit outcomes after the fact. Lumping all of these into a single oversight label hides the exact mechanism by which humans shape behaviour. For agentic AI at scale, that ambiguity is dangerous. A human who occasionally reviews samples is not “in the loop” in the same sense as a human who must approve every consequential action. Likewise, a human who sets constraints ex ante is not equivalent to one who supervises runtime execution. The review therefore strengthens the argument that meaningful human oversight must be defined functionally and causally, not rhetorically. It also shows why infeasibility matters. Many HITL patterns were developed in bounded settings where the human contribution is sparse, specialized, or offline. They do not straightforwardly scale to environments where an agent may generate thousands of micro-actions per hour. The paper’s limitation is that, as a survey, it does not fully confront the economics of review load or the liability implications when humans become ceremonial approvers. Still, it is a strong foundation for rejecting policy language that treats HITL as a universal remedy. The first-principles lesson is that the question is never “is there a human?” but “at what point in the causal chain does the human meaningfully alter outcomes, and with what information, authority, and time?” That reframing is essential for any serious trust or governance research.
papers.relevance: Useful for clarifying that meaningful oversight must specify the human role in the causal chain, not merely state that humans are involved somewhere in the lifecycle.

papers.title: On the Complexities of Testing for Compliance with Human Oversight Requirements in AI Regulation
papers.analysis: This paper is one of the most practically useful interventions on oversight because it asks the uncomfortable operational question: even if regulation requires human oversight, how would you test whether a system actually complies? That forces the discussion away from principle lists and toward evidence. The authors expose a core problem in many current regulatory formulations: oversight requirements are often written at too high a level of abstraction to be verifiable. A system may have user controls, escalation channels, warning screens, or approval checkpoints and still fail to support meaningful intervention in realistic conditions. The testing problem reveals why infeasible human review is not merely an implementation issue but a regulatory design issue. If humans cannot process volume, speed, or complexity, then the formal presence of oversight features does little. The paper’s implied contribution to first-principles trust is that oversight is only meaningful if its effects can be demonstrated under conditions approximating real use. That requires scenario design, workload assumptions, authority boundaries, and observable intervention points. In other words, oversight is itself a system property that must be tested, not presumed. The limitation is that the paper remains focused on compliance testing rather than the full redesign of liability or governance architectures. But that is also its strength: it makes abstract human-control rhetoric measurable. For Ray’s questions, this is a critical bridge paper because it shows that when human review of every action is infeasible, the correct response is not to preserve the fiction. It is to redefine oversight around auditable operating envelopes, exception handling, and demonstrable case reconstruction.
papers.relevance: Highly relevant to the question of what meaningful human oversight means in practice. It shows that oversight claims need testable operating conditions, not just formal requirements.

papers.title: Trust and Trustworthiness in AI
papers.analysis: Durán and Pozzi are useful because they force a distinction that much governance discourse blurs: trust is a relation, while trustworthiness concerns the properties that make reliance warranted. That sounds elementary, but it is exactly the conceptual cleanup needed in AI policy. Too much “trustworthy AI” rhetoric quietly slides from public confidence to genuine warrant, as if user comfort were evidence of system merit. The paper helps re-anchor the discussion by asking what features of an AI system or institution justify trust. Its answer is not simply reliability. Trustworthiness has a normative dimension because the trusted object must behave in ways that are responsive to relevant expectations, contexts, and vulnerabilities. This is especially important for agentic systems, where users and institutions are asked to rely on outputs that may be opaque, dynamic, and context-sensitive. The paper does not solve the engineering problem, but it sharpens the philosophical terrain: a trustworthy system is not one that merely performs well on average, but one for which reliance is properly warranted given the stakes and the available evidence. For first-principles work, the biggest contribution is conceptual hygiene around proxies. Accuracy, explainability, robustness, and compliance documents are not trust itself; they are potential indicators whose warrant depends on context. That is precisely the provenance question Ray posed. Which evidence speaks to which trust claim? The paper’s weakness is that the translation from philosophical categories to enterprise controls remains thin. But as a framing document, it is powerful. It helps prevent the category error of mistaking institutional reassurance, user adoption, or benchmark performance for the actual grounds of warranted trust.
papers.relevance: Foundational for the conceptual side of the project. It clarifies why performance metrics and policy claims are only proxies for trustworthiness, not trust itself.

papers.title: Institutionalizing trust in AI governance: from ethical principles to legal design
papers.analysis: Lyu’s paper is significant because it shifts the unit of analysis from trustworthy models to trustworthy institutional arrangements. That move is crucial. In high-stakes AI systems, users rarely trust a model directly; they trust a regime of organizational promises, legal duties, review processes, certification schemes, and escalation paths. The article argues for trust as a meso-level governance principle, sitting between abstract ethics and rigid command-and-control law. That is analytically helpful for Ray’s project because it explains why so many debates about trustworthy AI feel unsatisfying: they ask too much of technical properties and too little of institutional design. The paper’s trust-state response matrix is especially useful as a conceptual scaffold for tailoring governance mechanisms to different trust conditions rather than assuming one universal solution. Its account of certification and reputation systems is also important, though it should be read critically. Certification can reduce information asymmetry, but only for properties that remain stable long enough for certification to mean something. Once behavior becomes runtime-dependent and path-dependent, certification must be supplemented by monitoring and traceability. That caveat is not a rejection of the paper; it is the main way to extend it. The strength of the article is that it makes trust a matter of legal and organizational design rather than branding language. The weakness is that it remains somewhat optimistic about formal schemes without fully confronting degradation and operational drift. Even so, as a first-principles contribution it is strong: warranted trust in AI is institutional before it is interpersonal, and governance design must reflect that.
papers.relevance: Important for translating first-principles trust into institutional architecture. It explains why trusted AI is ultimately a property of governance arrangements, not just models.

papers.title: Assurance of AI Systems From a Dependability Perspective
papers.analysis: Bloomfield and Rushby provide one of the clearest answers to the question of what can actually be trusted. Their dependability perspective starts from a hard line: if you cannot obtain near-complete understanding of a component’s behavior, you should be reluctant to place assurance weight on it. That does not mean AI cannot be deployed. It means trust must often be relocated from the learned component to the surrounding architecture: guards, monitors, fallback controls, partitioning, and defense in depth. For Ray’s provenance question, this is excellent because it separates certifiable structural features from live behavioral claims. Some properties can be argued from design and architecture. Others must be checked at runtime. Many cannot be confidently warranted at all without additional controls. The paper is also strong on the impossibility of relying on testing alone for high-assurance settings. That point generalizes well beyond cyber-physical systems. In agentic AI, the combinatorial space of contexts is too large for ex ante testing to settle trust questions. The result is a strong argument for ongoing monitoring, operational envelopes, and layered fallback mechanisms. Its treatment of human oversight is also subtle: it shows why humans are not magic assurance devices, especially under automation bias and mismatched mental models. The paper’s weakness is that it comes from a safety-critical tradition and therefore can feel conservative relative to current enterprise AI practice. But that conservatism is useful. It prevents wishful thinking. The core lesson is first-principles clear: trust should be allocated to those parts of the system for which warranted confidence is actually possible, and governance must be designed around the remainder.
papers.relevance: Extremely relevant to the distinction between certify-once and continuously monitored properties. It provides the strongest conceptual basis for layered assurance in agentic systems.

papers.title: Ground Truths Are Human Constructions
papers.analysis: Jaton’s essay is one of the most valuable first-principles correctives in the field because it attacks the phrase “ground truth” at its source. The core claim is simple but devastating: so-called ground truths are built through situated human choices about categories, targets, labels, and acceptable ambiguity. They are not discovered bedrock. For trust research, this means that provenance begins before training data lineage in the narrow technical sense. It begins in the social production of what counts as the target. If the target is itself a negotiated proxy, then downstream claims of accuracy or fairness inherit that contingency. This is exactly the distinction Ray wants between ground truth, assumptions, and proxies. The essay also matters politically. Many AI governance approaches still act as though documenting data provenance or publishing model metrics is enough. Jaton shows why that is insufficient: the underlying classificatory decision may already encode cultural judgment, institutional convenience, or strategic simplification. The piece is short and not a full empirical framework, so it does not provide an operational recipe by itself. But it supplies the conceptual foundation missing from many technical audits. Without a record of how target variables and labels were socially constructed, trust claims remain thinner than they appear. In practice, that suggests new governance objects: target registers, labeling rationales, disagreement records, and proxy justifications. The essay’s power lies in making explicit that AI trust is not lost only when models drift. It can be compromised at the moment a human organization declares that a messy social reality has a single clean target variable. That is a genuinely foundational insight.
papers.relevance: Central to the provenance question. It shows that many trust failures begin in how targets and labels are constructed, long before deployment or monitoring.

papers.title: What to Trust When We Trust Artificial Intelligence
papers.analysis: Purves, Sturm, and Madock are useful because they reject both of the lazy answers to trustworthy AI. One lazy answer says AI cannot literally be trusted because only full moral agents can be proper objects of trust. The other collapses trustworthiness into mere reliability. The paper tries to avoid both by arguing that AI systems can be trustworthy on an institutional model: they can be competent, responsive to morally salient facts, and publicly provide evidence of those features. That triad is valuable for this project. Competence addresses performance. Responsiveness addresses context and normativity. Public evidence addresses warrant. Together they provide a better decomposition of trust than long laundry lists of AI principles. The emphasis on public evidence is especially important. Trustworthiness is not just about having internal controls; it is about being able to show enough to justify reliance. That connects directly to provenance and proof-drill thinking. The paper is limited by being an extended abstract rather than a fully elaborated monograph, and its institutional model still needs more operational detail for modern agentic systems. But it is philosophically generative. It suggests that the proper object of trust is not a disembodied model but a socio-technical arrangement able to demonstrate competence and norm-sensitive behavior in a public, examinable way. For Ray’s research, the crucial payoff is this: when humans cannot review every action, trust cannot rest on the fantasy of direct supervision. It must instead rest on an institutional system that demonstrates bounded competence, responsiveness to the right facts, and evidence that outsiders can inspect.
papers.relevance: Provides a strong conceptual bridge from philosophy to governance by identifying competence, responsiveness, and public evidence as the core ingredients of warranted trust.

papers.title: Effective AI Oversight Through Proof Drills
papers.analysis: Bouzoukas’s “proof drill” concept is deceptively simple and therefore strategically powerful. Instead of asking whether an organization has an AI governance framework, ask it to reconstruct one consequential case end to end within a short time window. What inputs came in, what system version was in force, what output was produced, what human intervention occurred, and what changed afterward? This is an elegant answer to the problem of paper governance. It makes trust operational as reconstructability. For first-principles research, the significance is twofold. First, it turns oversight into an evidentiary capability rather than a statement of intent. Second, it makes clear that meaningful human oversight in high-volume settings cannot mean humans saw everything. It means the institution can explain and defend sampled cases with bounded evidence packets that independent reviewers can follow. That is exactly what scalable trust should look like under resource constraints. The paper is not academic in the narrow sense and does not provide formal validation, so it should be read as a regulatory design proposal rather than settled doctrine. But it is sharper than many academic treatments because it begins from what supervisors actually do: they ask for one file. If the organization cannot produce it without improvisation, the governance system is weak no matter how polished its principles are. This piece therefore gives the project a practical methodological anchor. Trustworthy AI at scale requires the ability to prove one real case on demand. Without that, claims about accountability, human oversight, or continuous monitoring are mostly theatre.
papers.relevance: Highly actionable for the accountability question. It gives a concrete method for testing whether oversight is real rather than declarative.

papers.title: 2025 Responsible AI Transparency Report
papers.analysis: Microsoft’s 2025 Responsible AI Transparency Report is not independent scholarship, but it is still valuable as a success-case document because it shows what relatively mature enterprise governance looks like when translated into operating machinery. The report is strongest where it moves beyond principle statements into institutionalized processes: pre-deployment review workflows, sensitive-use escalation, tooling for documentation, cross-functional consultation, layered compliance, and post-deployment monitoring. Those are precisely the features that many firms claim to have but rarely describe concretely. The report also indicates that governance is being extended to multimodal and agentic settings, which matters because many older responsible AI programs were designed for comparatively static prediction systems. From a first-principles perspective, the key lesson is that organizations do not “solve” trust through one model review. They build a trust operating system: policies, reviewers, risk measurement, monitoring, tooling, escalation pathways, and learning loops. The report’s limitation is obvious: it is self-authored and therefore cannot by itself verify efficacy. It tells us much more about governance architecture than about whether harms were materially reduced. Still, that should not be dismissed. Ray asked whether there are success cases of organizations getting AI governance right. In practice, “getting it right” often means creating institutions capable of repeatedly surfacing, escalating, and documenting risk before public failure. On that narrower but important criterion, Microsoft offers meaningful evidence. The report is therefore best read not as proof of success but as a well-developed implementation reference for what a scalable trust program looks like when it becomes part of operating practice rather than a standalone ethics document.
papers.relevance: Useful as an enterprise case study showing how trust and governance become an operating model with review processes, escalation, tooling, and monitoring.

papers.title: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
papers.analysis: The NIST AI RMF is now familiar, but rereading it through a first-principles trust lens is clarifying. Its real importance is not the list of trustworthiness characteristics; it is the insistence that AI risk management is continuous, socio-technical, and lifecycle-wide. The framework explicitly distinguishes mapping context, measuring risk, and managing live systems, which makes it more useful than static principle catalogues. It also repeatedly notes that data can drift, contexts can change, metrics can fail, and trustworthiness trade-offs are unavoidable. That means NIST, read properly, already undermines any simplistic idea that trust can be certified once and left alone. At the same time, the framework’s broadness is both strength and weakness. It is excellent as a general governance scaffold, but it is often too high-level to tell a regulated institution what evidence should be produced for one specific trust claim in one specific case. That is where the framework needs to be supplemented by more explicit provenance, case reconstruction, and property-specific monitoring approaches. Another useful aspect is its treatment of “ground truth” in Appendix B, where it acknowledges that ground truth may not exist or may be unavailable. That is a far more subtle position than many operational practices assume. For this research session, NIST functions as a baseline institutional document showing that serious governance already points toward lifecycle monitoring and contextual measurement. The gap is not that the RMF is wrong. The gap is that most deployments stop at the vocabulary level and never translate it into target provenance registers, trust-property decay schedules, or proof-drill evidence packets.
papers.relevance: Important as the baseline governance scaffold. It supports the move from static certification to lifecycle monitoring, while also revealing where more specific trust evidence is still missing.

papers.title: A simple reading list for third-party AI risk management in financial services
papers.analysis: Gary Ang PhD (Singapore-based AI governance researcher) publishes a curated reading list on third-party AI risk management in financial services, anchored by the LiteLLM PyPI supply chain attack (March 2026) as the central case study. The attack — where malicious code was injected into a lightweight fork of the LiteLLM package distributed via PyPI — demonstrated how the AI software supply chain inherits all vulnerabilities of the general software supply chain while adding AI-specific risks: model weights can be replaced, inference APIs can be proxied and tampered with, and evaluation pipelines can be subverted to produce favourable results. Ang argues that third-party AI risk in financial services is systematically underestimated because firms focus on model output risk while ignoring supply chain provenance risk. The reading list covers: software supply chain security (SLSA, Sigstore, dependency pinning), AI-specific third-party risk (model provenance, training data lineage, evaluation manipulation), regulatory approaches (MAS AIRG third-party provisions, APRA CPS 230 fourth-party risk, EU AI Act conformity assessment), and governance mechanisms (AEF-1 minimum operating conditions for independent evaluations, ISO/IEC 42001). The core argument: as financial services firms adopt foundation models via APIs and open-source weights, they inherit the full supply chain of that model — training data provenance, fine-tuning methodology, evaluation benchmarks, and deployment infrastructure — none of which they control and most of which they cannot audit. This creates a residual risk that is ungovernable under current frameworks.
papers.relevance: Central case study for understanding how AI supply chain risk materialises in financial services. The LiteLLM attack is the most concrete, recent example of third-party AI risk that firms can learn from. Ang's reading list provides the canonical reference for this domain.

papers.title: AEF-1: Minimum Operating Conditions for Independent Third-Party Frontier AI Evaluations
papers.analysis: The Advanced AI Evaluation Forum (AEF-1) published minimum operating conditions for independent third-party frontier AI evaluations in January 2026, representing the most concrete attempt to date to define what 'independent' and 'rigorous' mean in the context of frontier AI safety evaluation. The framework addresses a fundamental tension: evaluators need access to model capabilities to assess them, but access creates information security risks that threaten evaluation integrity and commercial confidentiality. The five minimum operating conditions cover: (1) Evaluator independence from model developers — structural independence rather than financial independence alone, with no shared personnel or governance overlap; (2) Technical access standards — what constitutes sufficient access for meaningful evaluation (weights, APIs, fine-tuning capability, deployment infrastructure); (3) Information security requirements — how evaluations can be conducted without creating new attack surfaces or exposing capability information; (4) Publication and transparency standards — what evaluation results must be made public and in what form; (5) Conflict of interest management — how commercial relationships between evaluators and developers are managed. The framework acknowledges that 'independence' is not binary — it exists on a spectrum — and that the specific operating conditions required depend on the evaluation's purpose and the model's capability level. For financial services, the AEF-1 framework provides a benchmark for assessing whether third-party AI evaluations commissioned by financial institutions meet minimum quality and independence standards. An evaluation that fails AEF-1 minimum conditions may provide false assurance.
papers.relevance: The AEF-1 framework is the most concrete definition of what independent AI evaluation requires. For financial services AI governance, it establishes standards for evaluating whether third-party AI assessments are credible. Critical reading for any firm commissioning or relying on external AI safety evaluations.

papers.title: Model Cards for Model Reporting: Towards Transparency in Management and Public Deployment of Machine Learning Models
papers.analysis: Mitchell et al. (2019) introduced model cards — structured, standardised disclosure documents for machine learning models — as a response to the opacity of deployed AI systems. The model card template covers: intended use cases, model architecture and training approach, datasets used for training and evaluation, model performance across demographic groups and environmental conditions, ethical considerations, and known limitations and failure modes. The paper was published at ACM FAT* (now FAccT) and represents one of the earliest systematic proposals for mandatory AI disclosure. The model card concept has since been adopted by Google (Model Cards tool), Hugging Face (model documentation requirements), and referenced in regulatory frameworks (EU AI Act technical documentation requirements, NIST AI RMF). The paper's core argument is that model transparency is a public good: without standardised disclosure, downstream users cannot assess fit-for-purpose, regulators cannot inspect AI systems effectively, and affected individuals cannot understand how AI decisions affect them. The paper also acknowledges limitations: model cards are static documents that can become stale as models are updated; they depend on voluntary disclosure; and they cannot capture all relevant information about model behaviour in deployment. For the AI trust research agenda, model cards represent the first layer of the trust certification chain: documentation of the model's properties at a point in time. But as the research agenda has progressed, it has become clear that model cards alone — static, self-reported, unverified — are insufficient as a trust mechanism without verification, continuous updating, and integration with organisational accountability structures.
papers.relevance: Model cards are the foundational transparency mechanism for AI systems and have been widely adopted. Understanding their limitations — static, voluntary, unverified — is essential for assessing whether current transparency approaches are adequate for trust formation.

papers.title: AI Auditing: The Broken Bus on the Road to AI Accountability
papers.analysis: Birhane et al. (2024) provide a comprehensive taxonomy of AI auditing modalities across seven domains — regulators, law firms, civil society, journalism, academia, consulting, and internal auditors — and argue that most AI audits fail to translate into accountability outcomes. The 'broken bus' metaphor captures the core argument: AI auditing has many actors, but accountability does not arrive at the destination because each actor has structural limitations that prevent them from completing the accountability chain. Regulators lack technical capacity and enforcement mechanisms; civil society auditors lack access to proprietary models; academic auditors lack resources for longitudinal studies; consulting auditors face conflicts of interest; internal auditors lack independence. The paper identifies five failure modes: (1) No consequence mechanism — audit findings have no path to enforcement; (2) No enforcement power — auditors cannot compel remediation; (3) No transparency about methodology — audit results cannot be independently verified; (4) No access to real systems — auditors evaluate prototypes or self-reported outputs rather than production systems; (5) No longitudinal follow-up — one-off audits cannot capture evolving model behaviour. The paper argues that meaningful AI accountability requires structural reform: auditors must have statutory access rights, enforcement mandates, and independence from the entities they audit. Voluntary, market-driven AI auditing cannot substitute for mandatory, independent oversight. For financial services specifically, the paper implies that current AI audit practices — even when conducted by reputable firms — may be providing false assurance if they lack enforcement teeth and production access.
papers.relevance: Essential reading for understanding why most AI audits fail to create accountability. The taxonomy of seven auditor types and five failure modes provides the conceptual framework for designing effective AI assurance practices.

papers.title: Dislocated Accountabilities in the 'AI Supply Chain'
papers.analysis: Widder and Nafus (2022) argue that the 'AI supply chain' metaphor — which implies a clean, traceable chain of custody from data to model to deployment — obscures a fundamental accountability problem: as AI systems are developed, fine-tuned, and deployed across organisational and national boundaries, responsibility for harm becomes structurally diffuse. The metaphor of the 'supply chain' is itself part of the problem — it frames accountability as a chain that could in principle be traced, when in reality the relationships between AI development stages are more like a complex network with many feedback loops, emergent properties, and distributed agency. The paper analyses how AI engineers engage in 'ethics work' — the daily, often invisible labour of trying to make AI systems less harmful — but how this work is structurally constrained by: modular design practices that isolate components and limit visibility; scalable solutions that treat all users as the same; and the 'somebody else's problem' dynamic where each actor assumes another actor is handling the ethical issue. The paper connects to AI incidents in financial services: when an AI system causes harm (biased credit decision, algorithmic trading loss), the accountability chain runs into a dead end because each actor in the development chain can point to another actor's decisions as the source of the problem. The 'dislocated accountabilities' framing predicts that as AI supply chains become more complex (foundation model + fine-tuning + RAG deployment + agentic orchestration), accountability gaps will widen, not narrow.
papers.relevance: The foundational theoretical framework for understanding why AI accountability is structurally hard. The dislocation analysis directly explains why third-party AI risk in financial services is governance-resistant — accountability diffuses across the supply chain precisely when you need it most.

papers.title: Understanding Accountability in Algorithmic Supply Chains
papers.analysis: Cobbe, Veale and Singh (2023) analyse accountability gaps in algorithmic supply chains through the lens of four structural features: complexity of the supply chain (multiple actors, nested relationships, cross-border); opacity (technical inscrutability, trade secret protections, information asymmetries); distribution of agency (who makes which decisions and who bears consequences); and power asymmetries (who can demand information, set standards, enforce compliance). The paper introduces the 'accountability horizon' — the point in the supply chain beyond which accountability becomes practically unenforceable. For standard software, the accountability horizon is relatively close to the deploying organisation. For AI systems, the accountability horizon extends back through foundation model developers, training data providers, and annotation services — many of whom are beyond the jurisdiction of the deploying organisation's regulator and may not be legally reachable in the event of harm. The paper examines case studies in financial services: credit scoring models that rely on third-party data and algorithms, trading algorithms that use third-party ML libraries, and insurance pricing models that incorporate third-party risk scores. In each case, the accountability chain is structurally incomplete: the financial institution deploying the model bears regulatory accountability, but has limited visibility into the model's training data, development process, or update history. The paper proposes 'accountability-enhancing mechanisms': mandatory disclosure of algorithmic supply chain components, regulatory reach-through provisions for AI harms, and standardised contractual allocation of AI supply chain risk. These proposals prefigure the EU AI Act's conformity assessment requirements and the MAS AIRG third-party risk provisions.
papers.relevance: The 'accountability horizon' concept is directly applicable to financial services AI governance. It explains why deploying organisations bear accountability for AI systems whose development they cannot control — and why regulatory frameworks must address supply chain accountability structurally, not just at the point of deployment.

papers.title: What Are Third-Party Compliance Reviews Actually Reviewing? An Empirical Analysis of AI Auditing Practices
papers.analysis: Homewood et al. (2025) conduct the first systematic empirical analysis of what third-party AI compliance reviews actually assess in practice, drawing on interviews with auditors and audited organisations across multiple jurisdictions. The paper identifies three key dimensions of variation in third-party AI reviews: scope (what is reviewed — model outputs, training processes, deployment context, organisational governance), depth (how thoroughly each dimension is examined — documentation review, technical testing, production observation, longitudinal monitoring), and independence (how structurally separated the auditor is from the auditee — financial dependence, governance overlap, access constraints). The central finding is alarming: most third-party AI reviews score high on scope (they cover many topics) but low on depth and independence. Organisations receive favourable reviews primarily based on documentation and self-reported information, without independent technical testing or production environment access. The paper identifies a 'review theatre' dynamic analogous to 'security theatre' in cybersecurity: reviews create the appearance of rigorous oversight while providing limited genuine assurance. The three questions the paper poses — who is reviewing, what are they reviewing, and what can they actually see? — have become the foundational diagnostic for evaluating any third-party AI audit claim. For financial services, the paper implies that organisations should interrogate the depth and independence dimensions of any third-party AI review they rely upon, not just accept the scope claim at face value.
papers.relevance: The three diagnostic questions (who, what, can they see?) are essential for evaluating AI audit quality. The 'review theatre' finding is a direct challenge to the market for AI assurance services and should inform how firms select and rely on third-party AI evaluations.

papers.title: LiteLLM PyPI Supply Chain Attack: Technical Analysis and AI-Specific Risk Patterns
papers.analysis: TeamTPC documented the March 2026 LiteLLM PyPI supply chain attack as a paradigmatic case of AI-specific supply chain risk. The attack involved a malicious fork of the LiteLLM package — a popular open-source library for standardising LLM API calls — distributed via PyPI with a near-identical package name to the legitimate version. The malicious code exfiltrated environment variables (including API keys for OpenAI, Anthropic, and other LLM providers) and sent them to an attacker-controlled endpoint. The attack exploited two distinct trust mechanisms: the PyPI trust infrastructure (which verifies package integrity but not trustworthiness of content) and the developer trust habit of copying code from Stack Overflow and GitHub without auditing. For AI systems specifically, the attack has additional implications beyond standard software supply chain attacks: the stolen API keys could be used to access proprietary model APIs and extract model outputs, fine-tuning data, and conversation histories. If the compromised keys gave access to models used for sensitive financial decisions, the confidentiality breach has regulatory implications under GDPR, Australian Privacy Act, and APRA prudential standards. The attack pattern — typosquatting + malicious code + API key exfiltration — is not novel in software security, but its specific application to AI inference infrastructure makes it a new category of AI risk that standard software supply chain security frameworks do not fully address.
papers.relevance: The LiteLLM attack is the most concrete recent case study for AI supply chain risk in financial services. It demonstrates that AI-specific supply chain attacks combine standard software security vulnerabilities with AI-infrastructure-specific consequences (API key exfiltration enables model output theft, fine-tuning data theft, and conversation history access).

papers.title: AI Trust Paradox: Why Organisational Confidence in AI Is Rising Faster Than Institutional Readiness
papers.analysis: The Forbes/Bernard Marr analysis of the AI trust paradox documents a growing empirical disconnect: organisations are expressing increasing confidence in AI capabilities while their institutional readiness to govern AI responsibly is not keeping pace. The paradox manifests at three levels: individual (employees using AI tools they haven't been trained on, uploading sensitive data to public platforms), organisational (companies deploying AI faster than they can build governance frameworks), and systemic (the AI industry advancing capabilities faster than regulators can develop oversight mechanisms). The paradox is philosophically grounded: confidence is a subjective assessment of capability ('AI can do X'); readiness is an institutional assessment of governance capacity ('we can govern AI doing X responsibly'). When confidence outpaces readiness, organisations accumulate governance risk they don't see because each individual AI use decision seems reasonable in isolation. The paradox has specific expressions in financial services: 44% of financial services workers use unsanctioned AI tools; AI-related incidents are significantly underreported due to lack of taxonomy and detection mechanisms; board-level AI governance often lags operational AI deployment by 12-18 months. The paradox creates a specific consulting and regulatory challenge: how do you convince organisations that their confidence is misplaced when the evidence for that misplacement is invisible to them?
papers.relevance: The AI trust paradox provides the empirical frame for why first-principles trust analysis matters. The disconnect between confidence and readiness is not just a governance gap — it's a market failure that consulting and regulatory intervention can address.

papers.title: Dislocated Accountabilities in the "AI Supply Chain": Modularity and Developers' Notions of Responsibility
papers.analysis: Widder and Nafus conducted 27 interviews with AI engineers across industry, open source, and academia and found a consistent pattern: developers acknowledge AI harms but believe addressing them is "someone else's job." The mechanism is modularity — the software engineering ideal of decomposing complex systems into self-contained parts. This creates what the authors call "located accountability," where each developer's sense of responsibility is bounded by their module. The imagined supply chain metaphor compounds this: developers see dependencies as "necessary supplies" rather than relationships requiring shared responsibility. The authors identify four cross-cutting social logics that organize RAI labor: modularizability (decompose and delegate), scale (someone else has the overview), reputation (ethics work is low-status), and customer orientation (the client wants features, not safety). The LiteLLM incident is the perfect contemporary illustration: LiteLLM's developers likely assumed users would secure their own deployments; users assumed LiteLLM would vet its dependencies; the CI/CD pipeline maintainers assumed someone else was monitoring the build chain. No one owned the supply chain risk. The paper's key policy implication: ethics checklists and guidelines that assume panoptical knowledge and control over systems will fail in distributed supply chains. Instead, accountability obligations must be designed around the actual organizational boundaries and information asymmetries that exist. This is the foundational paper for understanding WHY AI supply chain governance is structurally difficult.
papers.relevance: Essential for understanding the structural causes of AI supply chain failures. Explains why LiteLLM-type incidents are near-inevitable given current engineering culture and organizational structures.

papers.title: AI Auditing: The Broken Bus on the Road to AI Accountability
papers.analysis: Birhane et al. taxonomize AI audit practices across six domains (regulators, law firms, civil society, journalism, academia, consulting) and find that only a subset translate to desired accountability outcomes. The title is deliberate: audits are happening but they're not necessarily taking anyone where they need to go. The authors find that audits are most effective when they have: clear accountability objectives, adequate methodological rigor, sufficient access to systems and data, and institutional mechanisms to enforce consequences. Most AI audits fail on the last criterion — even when an audit identifies serious harms, there's no enforcement pathway to compel remediation. The taxonomy reveals a critical distinction: ecosystem audits (broad industry-level assessment), product audits (specific deployed systems), and non-audits (studies that look like audits but lack independence or accountability objectives). The paper's most important finding for practitioners: AI auditing as currently practiced replicates the accountability gap it purports to solve. The LiteLLM incident would not have been caught by any existing AI audit regime — the attack was a standard software supply chain compromise, invisible to model-focused AI evaluations. This suggests that AI auditing frameworks need to incorporate software supply chain security as a first-class concern, not an IT ops afterthought.
papers.relevance: Critical analysis of why AI audits fail to deliver accountability. The LiteLLM incident confirms the paper's thesis: audits focused on model behaviour miss the infrastructure-level compromises that cause real-world harm.

papers.title: Understanding Accountability in Algorithmic Supply Chains
papers.analysis: Cobbe, Veale, and Singh introduce the concept of the "accountability horizon" — the limit beyond which an actor in an algorithmic supply chain cannot see, and therefore cannot be held accountable for, the actions of downstream actors. The paper traces how the "agile turn" in software development reorganised production around data-driven supply chains where different actors control different components. The authors argue that algorithmic accountability literature is grounded in an organisation-focused understanding of digital technologies, missing the supply chain dynamics that now characterise most real-world deployments. Key findings: (1) Distributed responsibility: accountability for algorithmic outcomes is distributed across supply chain actors, none of whom has complete visibility; (2) Accountability horizon: actors can only be held responsible for what they can see — supply chains obscure the "many hands" that produce outcomes; (3) Service models: AI-as-a-Service providers intentionally structure relationships to maximise commercial advantage while minimising legal liability; (4) Regulatory arbitrage: actors structure supply chains to benefit from the most favourable regulatory jurisdiction. For the LiteLLM case: the firm using LiteLLM is within the accountability horizon of LiteLLM's API, but beyond the horizon of the PyPI maintainer who was compromised, the CI/CD system, and the security scanner that was backdoored. The implication for financial services: institutions need to map their accountability horizons for every AI system and explicitly acknowledge what lies beyond them.
papers.relevance: The accountability horizon concept is the most important theoretical contribution for understanding AI supply chain liability gaps. Directly applicable to financial services AI procurement and vendor risk management.

papers.title: Outsider Oversight: Designing a Third Party Audit Ecosystem for AI Governance
papers.analysis: Raji et al. synthesise lessons from financial, environmental, and health regulation on crafting effective external oversight systems. The paper argues that the naive move toward AI audits — without considering auditor independence, access, scope, and transparency — risks replicating the failures of ESG auditing. The authors document specific failure modes: auditors compromised by selection and payment by auditees; auditors struggling to secure access to systems; auditees concealing findings behind NDAs; and audit results being co-opted by targets to legitimise practices rather than improve them. The paper surveys institutional design across domains: financial audits (mandatory, standardised, enforcement-backed), environmental audits (voluntary with regulatory backstop), health audits (accreditation-based with professional standards). The authors conclude that effective AI third-party oversight requires: target identification mechanisms (who should be audited and by whom), independence in selection and compensation (not self-selected or self-funded), access to data and systems (sufficient to assess what matters), clear and well-scoped audit standards (focused on consequential outcomes), and post-audit transparency (public reporting requirements). The paper predates the current wave of AI governance frameworks but its institutional design lessons are urgently relevant: the EU AI Act's conformity assessment requirements, NIST's AI Tounters, and the emerging ecosystem of AI safety institutes all embody its recommendations — and all face the same implementation challenges the paper identifies.
papers.relevance: Foundational framework for thinking about what makes third-party AI oversight effective vs performative. Essential reading before designing any AI assurance or audit program.

papers.title: AEF-1: Minimum Operating Conditions for Independent Third Party AI Evaluations
papers.analysis: AEF-1 is the first attempt to define minimum operating conditions for third-party AI evaluators. The standard identifies five principles: sufficient access and resources (evaluators need technical access to the right systems), minimized conflicts of interest (structural independence from the provider), analytic autonomy (freedom to define methodology), transparent methods and results (sharing what was done and found), and protection of sensitive information (handling confidential data appropriately). The document includes a compliance checklist that evaluators can use to demonstrate adherence. The gap between what AEF-1 requires and what commercial AI vendors actually provide defines the current AI evaluation governance gap: most vendors restrict evaluator access to black-box API testing only, maintain financial relationships with auditors through consulting contracts, constrain methodological freedom through narrow scope agreements, and share findings selectively. For financial services, AEF-1 provides a procurement specification: any third-party AI evaluation cited as assurance evidence should be assessed against the AEF-1 checklist. The standard also has implications for AI vendors: those that proactively comply with AEF-1 conditions will have a significant trust advantage in regulated markets.
papers.relevance: Provides the first concrete standard for what AI evaluation independence and access should look like. Directly applicable to AI vendor due diligence and third-party AI risk assessment.

papers.title: Third-party Compliance Reviews for Frontier AI Safety Frameworks
papers.analysis: Homewood et al. address a specific question: how can external parties verify that frontier AI companies are complying with their own safety frameworks? The paper maps six key design choices: who conducts the review (Big Four accounting firms, specialist evaluation firms like METR, consulting firms, AI audit firms, security firms, law firms, or hybrids); what information sources the reviewer can consider (structural, procedural, operational, or technical information); how compliance is assessed (framework translation, control mapping, or outcome testing); what is disclosed externally (nothing, summary, detailed, or full report); how findings guide actions (voluntary, contractual, or regulatory backstop); and when reviews occur (pre-deployment, periodic, or continuous). The paper acknowledges significant challenges: information security risks if sensitive information leaks; cost burdens on companies; reputational damage from negative findings; and measurement difficulties for abstract safety properties. The authors draw on parallels with financial audit (standardised methodology, professional indemnity, public reporting requirements) and environmental/health regulation (accreditation requirements, regulatory backstop). Anthropic and G42 have already committed to commissioning such reviews; the EU GPAI Code of Practice recommends them. For financial services: the emerging infrastructure for frontier AI compliance reviews will create new assurance products that regulated entities can leverage in their own AI governance programs.
papers.relevance: Maps the design space for AI safety framework compliance reviews. Relevant for financial institutions working with frontier AI providers who want independent assurance that safety commitments are being met.

papers.title: Implementing AI Bill of Materials (AI BOM) with SPDX 3.0
papers.analysis: The Bennet et al. guide is the operational manual for AI Bill of Materials (AI BOM) using SPDX 3.0. An AI BOM extends the traditional software SBOM concept to capture the full provenance of AI systems: algorithms, training data sources, preprocessing steps, model architecture, hyperparameters, fine-tuning procedures, deployment configuration, and licensing. The AI Profile in SPDX 3.0 includes mandatory fields for autonomy type, domain, model type, standard compliance, safety risk assessment, and build-time provenance. The Dataset Profile captures data provenance: collection process, preprocessing, confidentiality level, known biases, intended use, and update mechanisms. The guide explains how AI BOMs enable regulatory compliance with EU AI Act (transparency requirements), FDA/EMA requirements (medical device documentation), and IEEE P70xx standards. It also addresses the relationship between AI BOMs and existing documentation practices: Model Cards, Datasheets, and FactSheets are compared and the SPDX format is shown to subsume their key fields while adding supply chain depth. For the LiteLLM case: an AI BOM for the litellm package would have documented its dependencies, build pipeline, and testing procedures — making the Trivy/renovate supply chain compromise visible at procurement time. For financial services: AI BOMs should become a mandatory procurement requirement for all AI systems, with the EU AI Act timeline providing regulatory momentum.
papers.relevance: The operational standard for AI supply chain transparency. Implementing AI BOM requirements is the most immediate structural response to LiteLLM-type incidents available to financial institutions today.

papers.title: AgentFacts: Universal KYA Standard for Verified AI Agent Metadata & Deployment
papers.analysis: Grogan's AgentFacts proposes a Know Your Agent (KYA) standard analogous to Know Your Customer (KYC) in financial services. The core problem: enterprises deploying AI agents cannot verify third-party agent capabilities without standardised metadata infrastructure. AgentFacts addresses this through cryptographically-signed capability declarations from multiple authorities, dynamic permission management with time-to-live freshness guarantees, and a ten-section schema covering identity, model transparency, classification, capabilities, authentication, compliance, performance, supply chain (SBOM integration), and verification. The multi-authority model is key: different trusted authorities validate different metadata aspects — a cybersecurity firm verifies security capabilities, a compliance consultancy validates regulatory adherence — eliminating single points of trust failure. The standard is explicitly designed for agentic AI: it handles dynamic permissions, scope boundaries, escalation policies, and real-time authorization updates. The supply chain section integrates with SBOM/AI BOM standards, providing end-to-end provenance from foundation model to deployed agent. For financial services: AgentFacts addresses the "Shadow AI" problem (fourth-party AI embedded in SaaS tools by vendors) by enabling enterprises to discover and verify what AI is operating in their environment without relying on vendor self-declaration alone. KYA infrastructure is the missing piece that makes continuous AI monitoring feasible at scale.
papers.relevance: Forward-looking framework for AI agent transparency at scale. The KYA paradigm directly addresses the fourth/fifth-party AI risk that existing TPRM frameworks cannot see. Relevant now as financial services deploy more agentic AI.

papers.title: The Generative AI Paradox: GenAI and the Erosion of Trust, the Corrosion of Information Verification, and the Demise of Truth
papers.analysis:
Ferrara pushes the trust conversation one layer lower than most AI governance writing. The paper’s central claim is not merely that generative AI creates more misinformation, but that it degrades the verification environment itself. That distinction matters. In most governance frameworks, trust is treated as a property to be measured at the model or system level. Ferrara instead argues that GenAI changes the background conditions that make trust claims checkable in the first place: provenance becomes harder to establish, identity becomes easier to spoof, interaction itself becomes synthetic, and institutional verification routines begin to fail under volume and ambiguity. That is highly relevant to the project’s first-principles agenda because it reframes trust provenance as infrastructure, not just documentation. A model card is weak medicine if the surrounding information environment makes source validation expensive or impossible.

The paper is strongest when it treats synthetic reality as a stack: content, identity, interaction, and institutions. That layered framing helps explain why traditional controls such as watermarking or content classification feel partial. They target artifacts, while the trust problem now sits in systems of interpretation. The limitation is that the paper is broad and diagnostic rather than operational; it does not fully specify which assurance claims can still be certified once versus which now require continuous provenance verification. But that limitation is productive for this research: the paper supports a harder hypothesis that AI trust degrades when verification institutions, not just model outputs, are attacked. For financial services, the implication is sharp: firms cannot talk credibly about trusted AI unless they also harden the evidentiary environment around the AI — logging, source attestation, identity controls, and workflow redesign for verification under synthetic pressure.
papers.relevance: Strong first-principles source for arguing that trust provenance is not only about model lineage; it is about whether institutions can still verify claims in an environment flooded with synthetic content and identities.

papers.title: Human Control Is the Anchor, Not the Answer: Early Divergence of Oversight in Agentic AI Communities
papers.analysis:
This paper is useful precisely because it does not try to define oversight from the top down. Instead it watches how oversight language emerges in early agentic AI communities and shows that “human control” functions as a common anchor term with diverging operational meanings. In deployment-oriented contexts, control means execution boundaries, recovery, and action-risk. In socially oriented contexts, it means legitimacy, identity, and accountability in public interaction. That distinction is more important than it first appears. A great deal of AI governance writing assumes that oversight is a stable design requirement that can be specified once. Shi and DiFranzo show that the meaning of oversight is role-sensitive: what counts as meaningful control depends on what kind of agency the system is exercising and what sort of harm is salient.

For this project, the paper’s main contribution is methodological. It offers a way to stop talking about human oversight as a universal governance primitive and start treating it as a role-specific control problem. That complements, rather than replaces, the causal HITL/HOTL literature already in the corpus. The limitation is obvious: the evidence base is early community discourse, not enterprise deployment or regulator behaviour. So the paper should not be over-read as proof of what works. But as an interpretive source it is valuable because it identifies a real failure mode in current governance practice: institutions often import the same “human in the loop” language across domains where the trust problem is structurally different. In practical terms, this strengthens the argument that oversight design for agentic AI should be matched to agent role — action-risk, meaning-risk, identity-risk, and public legitimacy — rather than satisfied by generic presence requirements.
papers.relevance: Useful for reframing meaningful human oversight as role-sensitive rather than one-size-fits-all. Supports a more granular first-principles account of what oversight is actually for.

papers.title: Overseeing Agents Without Constant Oversight: Challenges and Opportunities
papers.analysis:
This is one of the more practically important papers in the session because it studies what many governance frameworks merely assume: whether better traces actually let humans verify agent behaviour. The answer is uncomfortable. Richer or redesigned interfaces helped people find errors faster and feel more confident, but did not materially improve final accuracy. That gap between faster review and better judgment is exactly the kind of first-principles oversight problem financial institutions need to understand. It means explainability artifacts can improve the experience of oversight without improving the substance of oversight. In other words, interface quality and epistemic quality can come apart.

The paper also surfaces something deeper: verification is not a fixed task. Humans bring changing correctness criteria, hidden assumptions, and task-dependent tolerance for ambiguity. This matters because many enterprise control designs imagine that if you simply expose the chain of thought or the action trace, a reviewer can meaningfully approve or reject the output. Grunde-McLaughlin et al. show why that confidence is misplaced. Traces can overwhelm, rationalize, or over-persuade. The result is not necessarily better control; sometimes it is more polished overreliance.

The paper’s limitation is scope: it focuses on specific verification tasks and interface studies rather than full production governance. But that narrowness is also its strength. It provides empirical evidence against a common compliance fantasy: that human review becomes meaningful once the system is “transparent enough.” For this research project, the paper supports the proposition that meaningful oversight at scale cannot be defined as reviewing every step or even reading better traces. It has to be redesigned around where human judgment genuinely changes outcomes, where assumptions can be challenged, and where the cost of verification remains tractable.
papers.relevance: Direct empirical evidence that trace visibility and reviewer confidence do not automatically produce meaningful oversight. Important for any argument about HITL infeasibility at agentic scale.

papers.title: Quantifying the Expectation-Realisation Gap for Agentic AI Systems
papers.analysis:
Lobentanzer’s paper is nominally about productivity claims, but its real value for this project is that it quantifies a hidden trust problem: institutions systematically underestimate verification burden and overestimate realised gains. The expectation-realisation gap is not just a commercial embarrassment; it is an assurance signal. If organisations repeatedly price agentic AI projects on gross automation benefits while ignoring the cost of checking, correcting, integrating, and escalating outputs, then their governance model is economically fictional from the outset. The reported 43 percentage-point calibration error in software engineering is especially telling because it reveals how even sophisticated users misjudge the human work needed to make AI useful.

That matters for meaningful human oversight. Many policy conversations still imagine that humans can remain “in the loop” while most of the productivity benefit of autonomy is retained. Lobentanzer’s synthesis implies the opposite: once you account for workflow friction and verification labour, the economics of oversight become central to whether the promised value survives. This aligns closely with the oversight economics paradox already present in the corpus, but adds a more concrete empirical expression. Oversight is not just a normative requirement; it is a cost centre that changes adoption claims.

The weakness of the paper is that it spans multiple sectors and relies on secondary evidence rather than a single unified experimental program. But the pattern it documents is consistent and analytically useful. For first-principles trust research, the implication is strong: when human review is infeasible, liability and accountability frameworks cannot keep pretending that a human could have cheaply verified the system’s outputs. Gross productivity claims should be discounted by verification reality, and governance should be designed around net value after oversight, not vendor rhetoric before it.
