papers.relevance: Excellent bridge between trust theory and operating reality. Shows that meaningful oversight is constrained not only by cognition but by economics and workflow integration.

papers.title: Human-Certified Module Repositories for the AI Age
papers.analysis:
Enyedi’s proposal for Human-Certified Module Repositories is one of the more concrete architectural responses to the trust provenance problem emerging from the LiteLLM and accountability-horizon work. The core insight is simple: as AI systems are increasingly assembled from modules, generated glue code, and agent-mediated composition, trust cannot rest on the final output alone. It has to be inherited from curated, reviewable, provenance-rich building blocks. That is important because much of the AI trust discussion still assumes that the model is the main object of assurance. Enyedi instead focuses attention on the module ecosystem from which AI-assembled systems are composed.

This paper complements the prior supply-chain session rather than duplicating it. The earlier work established the structural problem: accountability diffuses across opaque software and model supply chains. Enyedi offers a partial control response: constrain composition through repositories of modules with stronger certification, interface contracts, and provenance metadata. For trust research, that matters because it suggests a practical answer to a first-principles question: what can be certified once? Certified modules, contracts, and provenance metadata are much better candidates for one-off assurance than end-to-end behaviour under open-world deployment conditions.

The paper is more architectural than evidentiary; it does not prove that HCMRs solve compositional risk in practice. There is also a risk of certification theatre if repositories become bureaucratic stamps rather than living assurance mechanisms. But the proposal is still highly relevant. It sharpens the distinction between certifying components and monitoring system behaviour, and it provides a useful design pattern for high-assurance environments where unconstrained AI composition should not be tolerated.
papers.relevance: A concrete architectural response to trust provenance and supply-chain opacity. Helpful for distinguishing what can be certified once from what must be monitored continuously.

papers.title: Unbounded Harms, Bounded Law: Liability in the Age of Borderless AI
papers.analysis:
Tran makes a move that much AI governance literature avoids: taking ex post liability seriously as a structural design problem rather than as an afterthought to ex ante risk management. The paper argues that AI harms are increasingly transboundary, supply-chain embedded, and jurisdictionally misaligned, which makes territorially bounded liability regimes inadequate. That directly advances this project’s third research question on how liability should be restructured when per-action human review is infeasible. If responsibility is already diffused across developers, deployers, integrators, data infrastructures, and cross-border service providers, then insisting on localised fault attribution to a final human reviewer becomes even less plausible.

The comparative method is valuable. By drawing on vaccine compensation schemes, nuclear liability, systemic financial risk governance, and environmental regimes, Tran shows that there are existing legal design patterns for harms that are difficult to localise: strict liability, pooled compensation, channelled liability, collective risk sharing. The significance is not that AI should copy any one regime wholesale, but that the law has tools for situations where causal complexity outruns ordinary negligence logic. That matters for agentic AI at scale, where action volume and supply-chain complexity make individualized human review or pinpoint attribution increasingly unrealistic.

The paper is still high-level and institutional. It does not solve the political feasibility problem, nor does it specify how compensation architecture would interact with regulatory supervision in financial services. But analytically it is strong. It supports the emerging hypothesis that liability frameworks for AI will need to move away from pure traceability and toward structured mechanisms for foreseeable but difficult-to-attribute harm. In practical terms, that means accountability should attach more heavily to design choices, deployment decisions, and compensation readiness than to the fiction that every harmful act could have been individually overseen by a human.
papers.relevance: Important source for restructuring liability beyond simple negligence and reviewer blame. Helps connect first-principles trust analysis to compensatory and institutional design.

papers.title: Epistemic Deference to AI
papers.analysis:
Lange’s paper is philosophically valuable because it addresses a question that often sits beneath practical governance debates without being named: when, if ever, should AI output replace a human’s independent reasons for belief? His answer is a rejection of strong preemption. Even when AI systems function as highly reliable artificial epistemic authorities, their outputs should contribute to a user’s evidence rather than displace it wholesale. That matters for this project because it provides a rigorous epistemic basis for skepticism about naïve automation. Meaningful oversight is not justified simply because a regulator likes human involvement; it is justified because full preemption by opaque systems creates epistemic pathologies — entrenchment, uncritical deference, and failure to notice when reliability conditions no longer hold.

The strongest contribution is that Lange ties deference to conditions of justified reliance rather than to anthropomorphic trust. This aligns well with the project’s existing distinction between trust and reliance. It also gives a cleaner way to speak about human oversight. The role of the human is not to ceremonially approve outputs but to preserve the broader evidence base against collapse into one privileged machine source. In that sense, the paper supports bounded human control at points where the human still has independent reasons, domain context, or authority to challenge the system.

Its practical weakness is that it remains abstract. The paper does not tell institutions how to operationalise the total-evidence view under real workflow constraints. But that is precisely why it is useful here: it identifies the epistemic standard, while other operational papers in this session show how hard it is to satisfy. Together they imply that if humans no longer have the time or evidence to add anything independent, then calling the arrangement “meaningful oversight” is conceptually strained.
papers.relevance: Strong first-principles paper for grounding oversight in epistemic theory rather than compliance rhetoric. Useful for distinguishing contributory review from wholesale machine preemption.

papers.title: Designing Meaningful Human Oversight in AI: Enhancing Human Agency in AI-Enabled Public Administration
papers.analysis:
Zhu’s CSIRO material is valuable because it translates the abstract slogan of “meaningful human oversight” into a design problem built around agency, solve–verify asymmetry, and system-level understanding. The crucial move is to separate operative agency from evaluative agency. AI may increasingly hold operative agency — generating solutions, taking actions, completing tasks — while humans must retain evaluative agency: the capacity to understand, judge, verify, redirect, or substitute. That distinction is sharper and more useful than generic HITL language because it identifies what human control must actually preserve when per-step intervention is no longer realistic.

The solve–verify asymmetry is the most practically important idea in the report. Some tasks are hard to solve but easy to verify; others are easy to generate but hard to verify. Meaningful oversight depends on recognising which regime you are in. That is a first-principles answer to the user’s question about what human-in-the-loop means at scale: it cannot mean reviewing everything uniformly. It must mean designing systems so that human review is concentrated where verification is feasible, consequential, and decision-changing, while hard-to-verify contexts trigger stronger constraints, abstention, routing, or redesign.

The report is more framework than evidence base, and parts of it read like a policy-oriented design deck rather than a fully argued academic paper. But it is still highly usable. The proposed mechanisms — boundary registries, provenance capture, confidence-aware routing, abstention criteria, drift monitoring, and external explanation packs — amount to a practical blueprint for converting “human oversight” from a ceremonial phrase into bounded intervention architecture. For this project, it is one of the clearest operational bridges between philosophical trust questions and deployable governance design.
papers.relevance: One of the clearest operational frameworks found for moving from generic HITL rhetoric to bounded intervention design. Highly relevant to agentic AI at scale.

papers.title: Epistemology gives a Future to Complementarity in Human-AI Interactions
papers.analysis: This paper is a rigorous philosophical treatment of why human-AI complementarity is harder to achieve than the popular literature suggests. Ferrario and Facchini argue that complementarity (a team of human plus AI outperforming both alone) is not guaranteed by merely having both agents present. Critically, complementarity is an ex-post property: you only know whether it held after you have ground truth against which to evaluate joint performance. At decision time, you cannot know whether the human-AI team is in a complementary regime. This has profound implications for trust governance. The paper applies computational reliabilism (a naturalistic epistemology from Goldman) to AI systems, arguing that AI trustworthiness should be assessed at the process level — whether the system follows reliable processes — rather than at the output level. This distinction maps directly onto the provenance question: some trust properties (process architecture, training data governance, interpretability) are knowable at design time; others (alignment stability, calibration drift, robustness under distribution shift) require continuous post-deployment process monitoring. The authors correctly identify that alignment is not a binary state but a family of properties that can degrade differently over time. For agentic AI, this means: trust architecture must decompose trust properties by their temporal degradation profile, and continuous monitoring is not optional for dynamic properties — it is the only mechanism available. This is one of the strongest philosophical foundations found for a first-principles approach to AI trust provenance.
papers.relevance: Provides the epistemological foundation for why trust properties must be decomposed by temporal degradation profile. Core reading for first-principles of trust.

papers.title: Epistemological Fault Lines Between Human and Artificial Intelligence
papers.analysis: This paper identifies seven fundamental epistemic differences between human and AI cognition: grounding, parsing, experience, motivation, causal reasoning, metacognition, and values. Each constitutes a 'fault line' — a domain where the two cognitive systems diverge structurally rather than just parametrically. The most critical for AI trust is Grounding: humans have embodied, situated, experiential understanding rooted in physical and social reality; LLMs have statistical pattern recognition over token distributions. This means LLMs can produce linguistically coherent outputs that are epistemically disconnected from the reality they describe. The authors introduce the concept of 'Epistemia' — a condition where AI produces linguistically plausible but epistemically ungrounded claims that appear credible because they mimic the surface features of well-reasoned discourse. This is not the same as hallucination (fabricated content) or calibration failure (miscalibrated confidence). It is a structural property of LLMs: their outputs are fluent and confident because language model training rewards fluency and confidence, not because the model has access to or correctly processed relevant ground truth. The implications for trust are severe. First, standard evaluation benchmarks cannot detect Epistemia because they measure surface agreement, not epistemic grounding. Second, the fault lines are not fixable by scaling alone — they are architectural properties of the token-prediction paradigm. Third, humans interacting with LLMs tend to project human-style epistemic evaluation onto the system because the outputs simulate epistemic discourse. For financial services, this means: model cards and performance metrics that look good on benchmarks may be measuring linguistic plausibility, not genuine epistemic reliability. The seven fault lines provide a first-principles taxonomy for decomposing trust into components that can and cannot be certified once.
papers.relevance: Provides the foundational taxonomy for understanding what AI cannot do epistemically. Essential for the provenance of trust question.

papers.title: Epistemic Compression: The Case for Deliberate Ignorance in High-Stakes AI
papers.analysis: This is a landmark paper that reframes the fundamental question of AI reliability in high-stakes domains. The authors identify a 'Fidelity Paradox': massive neural networks succeed in stable environments (protein folding, game playing) but fail where reliability matters most (medicine, finance, policy). The failure mode is not insufficient data — it is overfitting to noise in non-stationary environments. The paper introduces a critical distinction between the 'Stable Regime' (objective ground truth exists, data is stationary — complexity works) and the 'Shifting Regime' (ground truth is non-stationary, data is scarce relative to complexity — simplicity wins). In the Shifting Regime, which characterises most financial services applications, the authors demonstrate that high-capacity models degrade MORE than simple models under distribution shift. A simple logistic regression on FICO/DTI features actually improved over time in the LendingClub reanalysis; a Feature-TokenTransformer degraded the most. This directly challenges the industry's assumption that more sophisticated models are more trustworthy. The 'Efficient Lesson' — that in Shifting Regime domains, domain expert priors beat raw compute — explains why human oversight is not just a governance constraint but an information-theoretic requirement. The paper also introduces the 'Fluency-Fidelity Gap': models optimised for human preference (RLHF) achieve rhetorical alignment (sounding plausible) rather than epistemic alignment (knowing when they don't know). For trust provenance: in Shifting Regime domains, training performance is not just an unreliable proxy for trust — it is an anti-proxy that inversely correlates with robustness under real-world conditions.
papers.relevance: Core paper for understanding why training performance is not a valid trust signal in financial services. First-principles argument for continuous monitoring.

papers.title: Redefining the Standard of Human Oversight for AI Negligence
authors: Harvard Journal of Law and Technology
papers.source: Harvard Journal of Law and Technology (JOLT) Digest
papers.analysis: This Harvard JOLT analysis examines how negligence law must be redefined for AI systems where per-action human review is infeasible. The core argument: traditional negligence requires establishing (1) duty of care, (2) breach, (3) causation, and (4) damages. When AI agents take thousands of actions per second, the 'breach' and 'causation' elements become temporally intractable for individual actions. The paper argues that courts are moving toward a 'reasonable AI system' standard — what a reasonable organisation would have done in terms of AI governance, oversight architecture, and monitoring — rather than asking whether any individual AI action was adequately reviewed. This shifts liability from individual action review to systemic governance adequacy. The paper is particularly valuable on the foreseeability question: courts increasingly treat AI's capacity for post-deployment adaptation as a 'known category of risk' that organisations must anticipate and govern, not an excuse that defeats liability. The development risk defence (the 'defect was unknown and unknowable') is weakening for machine learning because the premise of ML is that unexpected behaviors are a predictable category. The practical standard emerging: organisations must implement robust fail-safes, real-time monitoring, rollback capabilities, and documented governance for autonomous AI. For this project's liability question: the answer is that liability is shifting from individual action review to systemic governance adequacy, and this is already reflected in how courts are treating AI negligence claims.
papers.relevance: Direct analysis of how negligence law handles AI when per-action review is infeasible. Essential for liability restructuring question.

papers.title: Operationalizing Trust: Human-in-the-Loop AI at Enterprise Scale
papers.analysis: This article provides a practitioner's operationalization of HITL for enterprise agentic AI, grounded in aviation's Crew Resource Management (CRM) analogy. The core argument: most organisations confuse 'presence' with 'practice' when it comes to human oversight. They deploy someone 'in the loop' without training them on what to approve, when to escalate, or how to recognise automation complacency. The aviation analogy is instructive. After the 1970s-80s accident wave, airlines redesigned pilot oversight through CRM: structured briefings, standard phraseology, challenge-and-response checklists, simulator-based training, and no-blame debriefs. Enterprise AI is at the same inflection point. The paper proposes five specific practices: (1) structured briefings before high-risk runs, (2) challenge-and-response for approvals requiring positive acknowledgment of intent/data lineage/permissions/blast-radius/rollback plan, (3) two-factor judgment for critical actions, (4) time-boxed decision lanes matched to risk (15s for low-risk, 15min for financial disbursements), (5) no-blame post-mission debriefs. These are directly implementable. The key insight for the human-in-the-loop-at-scale question: oversight only works where it has been practiced under realistic stress. A nominal approver who has never faced a genuine escalation decision under pressure is not a meaningful control — they are a liability dressed as governance. This requires simulation-based training, not documentation-based compliance.
papers.relevance: Most concrete implementation guidance found for operationalizing meaningful human oversight at enterprise scale.

papers.title: Agency Reimagined: Liability in an era of Agentic AI
papers.analysis: This Australian legal analysis is directly relevant to the supervisor's liability question because it examines how existing common law doctrines handle agentic AI without waiting for dedicated legislation. Three points are particularly valuable. First, apparent authority: Australian common law holds that objective intention creates authority — internal limits on an AI agent's authority (policies, instructions) are irrelevant unless communicated to third parties. This means an organisation deploying an agentic AI may be bound by its representations to third parties even if those representations exceeded internal instructions. Second, the Air Canada chatbot precedent: a Canadian tribunal rejected Air Canada's argument that the chatbot was 'merely a tool' and held the airline responsible for its representations — establishing that organisations cannot disclaim responsibility for AI-generated content. Third, directors' duties: Australian regulators have consistently held that reliance on technology does not dilute accountability, and directors are expected to understand at a high level how agentic systems operate and what risks they introduce. For liability restructuring: the paper argues that existing Australian law applies directly to agentic AI, but the non-deterministic behavior of agentic AI complicates foreseeability and control questions that are central to negligence duty of care. The answer for financial services is that organisations need explicit authority mapping (what decisions can agents take autonomously vs. what requires human approval), stress-tested legal risk scenarios, and identity governance that technically enforces oversight checkpoints.
papers.relevance: Most directly applicable Australian legal analysis for agentic AI liability. Clarifies how existing law applies without needing new legislation.

papers.title: Who is responsible when AI acts autonomously and things go wrong?
papers.source: Global Legal Insights — AI, Machine Learning & Big Data Laws and Regulations
papers.analysis: This chapter provides a comprehensive cross-jurisdictional analysis of AI liability, covering the UK, EU, and US. Three findings are directly relevant to this project. First, responsibility gaps: every stakeholder in the AI supply chain — developers, data curators, compliance teams, corporate executives — has plausible deniability, and victims can end up with no clear defendant. The proposed solutions include channeling liability to a single entity (like nuclear law channels it to the operator, with mandatory insurance), mandatory pre-market certification for high-risk AI, and layered responsibility frameworks. Second, the development risk defence is weakening: the premise of ML is that unexpected behaviors are a predictable category, so 'the defect was unknowable' is increasingly implausible for deployed AI systems. Third, the EU approach is the most advanced: the revised Product Liability Directive extends to AI software, courts can order disclosure of technical documentation to address the black-box challenge, and the AI Act imposes ongoing post-market monitoring obligations. The UK approach is sector-specific and principles-based. The US approach is fragmented by state and relies on traditional doctrines. For financial services operating across jurisdictions: the EU model (mandatory certification, disclosure obligations, post-market monitoring) represents the most demanding compliance standard and is likely to become the de facto global standard for multinationals.
papers.relevance: Comprehensive cross-jurisdictional liability analysis. Identifies the responsibility gap as the central legal challenge of agentic AI.

papers.title: Artificial Intelligence (AI) Model Risk Management
papers.analysis: MAS published this information paper following a thematic review of banks' AI model risk management practices in mid-2024, covering both traditional AI and Generative AI. The paper sets out observed good practices across three domains: governance and oversight; key risk management systems and processes; and development and deployment of AI. The governance section emphasises board and senior management accountability for AI model risk, clear ownership and escalation paths, and regular reviews of AI risk appetite. The risk management section covers model validation, monitoring for performance degradation, and incident management. The development and deployment section covers data quality, model documentation, and change management. What makes this notable as a success case: MAS did not wait for perfect frameworks before acting — it used its existing supervisory powers to conduct a thematic review, documented what good practice actually looked like across institutions, and published it as guidance that the industry could self-assess against. This is regulatory empiricism: building governance guidance from observed field practice rather than theoretical framework design. This is directly relevant to the supervisor's question about success case studies. MAS is one of the few regulators that has actually observed AI model risk management practices at scale and published findings.
papers.relevance: Most concrete success case study of regulator-led AI governance improvement. MAS thematic review methodology is a model for other regulators.

papers.title: A New Framework for Understanding Trust, Trustworthiness, and Reliance in AI Systems
papers.analysis: This is the first comprehensive philosophical survey of the trust/trustworthiness/reliance distinction as applied to AI. The paper makes several critical contributions. First, it provides the definitive philosophical articulation of the trust/trustworthiness distinction: 'Trust is an attitude we have towards people whom we hope will be trustworthy, where trustworthiness is a property, not an attitude' (following McLeod). Trustworthiness is the objective property; trust is the subjective stance. For AI, this means the question 'do we trust AI?' conflates two separate questions: (1) does the AI have trustworthiness properties? (2) do humans choose to trust it? These can come apart in both directions — an untrustworthy AI may be trusted, and a trustworthy AI may be distrusted. Second, the paper maps the philosophical literature on extra factors in trustworthiness: transparency, explainability, accountability, and fairness are all candidates for the 'extra factor' that converts mere reliability into trustworthiness. Third, the paper engages computational reliabilism (Goldman) as a naturalistic theory of trustworthiness: a system is trustworthy if it follows processes that reliably produce true outputs in relevant environments. This connects to the provenance question: reliability in the ML sense (high accuracy on training distribution) is not the same as reliability in the philosophical sense (processes that produce true outputs in the actual deployment environment). The paper's framework directly supports the argument that trust properties decompose into components with different epistemic status.
papers.relevance: First philosophical survey of AI trust/trustworthiness/reliance. Provides the conceptual foundation for the trust provenance question.

papers.title: Human-in-the-Loop: A 2026 Guide to AI Oversight That Actually Works
papers.analysis: This practitioner's guide distinguishes three oversight modes that are critical for thinking clearly about human-AI relationships at scale. HITL (human-in-the-loop): human must approve before action — appropriate for high-risk decisions. HOTL (human-on-the-loop): AI acts autonomously, human monitors and can intervene after — appropriate for medium-risk, reversible scenarios. HOT (human-out-of-the-loop): full automation — appropriate for low-risk, high-volume actions. The critical insight is that agentic AI workflows require dynamic switching between these modes based on risk context — a single workflow can require all three oversight levels at different steps. For example, an AI agent booking travel: autonomously rebook economy passengers (HOT), but escalate to human for first-class international reissues (HITL), while a supervisor monitors for anomalies across all rebookings (HOTL). The paper's five specific operational practices are the most implementable found: structured pre-run briefings, challenge-and-response checklists requiring positive acknowledgment, two-factor judgment for critical actions, time-boxed decision lanes by risk tier, and no-blame post-incident debriefs. The aviation analogy is extended: aviation doesn't just specify that pilots should be 'in the loop' — it specifies what that means operationally through CRM training, simulator practice, and standardized procedures. Enterprise AI governance needs the same operational specificity.
papers.relevance: Most practically useful framework for implementing risk-tiered human oversight at scale across different AI action types.

papers.title: Ground Truths Are Human Constructions
papers.analysis: Jaton, an STS scholar embedded with AI teams for 30 months, demonstrates that ground truth datasets are not objective facts but socially negotiated constructions bearing the cultural fingerprints of the annotators who created them. This is a foundational critique for AI trust research: if ground truth is constructed, then AI models trained on it inherit those construction biases. The "raw data" myth is debunked. Data is always labeled, curated, and shaped by human decisions. This means AI trust cannot rest on the fiction of objective ground truth — it must rest on the governance of the construction process itself. For financial services, this means: who labeled the training data? What assumptions did they embed? How was disagreement resolved? The provenance of the training data is as important as the model architecture. This paper is essential reading for anyone attempting to establish trustworthy AI: the epistemic foundation of trust in AI is itself socially constructed, not mechanically guaranteed.
papers.relevance: Foundational first-principles paper on AI epistemology. Provides the philosophical grounding for why AI trust cannot rest on "ground truth" as objective fact — ground truth is always constructed, negotiated, and power-laden. Critical for understanding the provenance question.

papers.title: Artificial Goodwill and Human Vulnerability: The Case for Building Merely Reliable, Rather than Trustworthy, Artificial Intelligence Technologies
papers.analysis: Carroll builds on Annette Baier's Goodwill Theory of Trust to argue that while AI systems can be built to be trustworthy according to three definitions of trustworthiness (competence, motive, and goodwill), doing so is actually undesirable because it creates unique human vulnerability. Baier's key insight is that trust involves making oneself vulnerable to another party's goodwill — the motivation to act in the trustor's interests, not just competently but intentionally. AI lacks goodwill in this sense: it has no motives, no capacity for genuine care, no standing to make moral commitments. Designing AI to be trustworthy in Baier's sense means humans become vulnerable to something that cannot genuinely care about them. Carroll argues we should build merely reliable AI — AI that performs its function competently without generating the false expectation of genuine care. The practical implication for financial services is significant: if you market your AI as "trustworthy" in the full sense of the term, you are creating a trust relationship your AI cannot reciprocate. This exposes your organisation to liability when the AI "betrays" that trust by failing in ways that, had it been a human trustee, would constitute bad faith.
papers.relevance: Critically important philosophical paper that challenges the AI industry's dominant framing of "trustworthy AI." Uses Baier's goodwill theory to argue we should aim for reliability not trustworthiness, as only the latter creates proper trust obligations. Essential for liability and accountability frameworks.

papers.title: Human trust in AI: a relationship beyond reliance
papers.analysis: Blanco argues against the dominant "reliance not trust" position (exemplified by Ryan 2020), contending that the key features of trusting relationships — vulnerability, normative expectations, possibility of betrayal — apply to human-AI relationships. Against the view that AI can only be relied upon (like a calculator), Blanco argues AI's unprecedented ability to replicate human decision-making, combined with its outputs being unforeseeable even to designers, creates genuinely new kinds of relationships. This matters for governance: if human-AI relationships are genuinely trusting relationships, then the governance obligations are different from those applying to reliable tools. Trust implies normative expectations beyond mere performance. Blanco's argument creates a conceptual challenge: if we accept that humans do genuinely trust AI systems (not just rely on them), then the question becomes whether the organisations deploying those systems can bear the weight of that trust. For financial services regulators, this suggests that organisations deploying AI in high-stakes decisions are not just selling a reliable tool — they are mediating trust relationships that create genuine obligations.
papers.relevance: Conceptually important paper that challenges the clean "reliance not trust" distinction used by many AI governance frameworks. If trust relationships genuinely exist between humans and AI, this has significant implications for how organisations must frame their AI governance obligations.

papers.title: Human-controllable AI: Meaningful Human Control
papers.analysis: Xu et al. provide a systematic analysis of Meaningful Human Control (MHC) in AI, articulating it as a unity of human understanding, intervention, and traceability of responsibility — requiring design, governance, and human participation together. Key for agentic AI: they argue that MHC requires human controllability as a prerequisite for future AI systems, operationalised through three pillars: (1) technical architectures with embedded human control mechanisms; (2) human-AI interactions optimised for human understanding; (3) AI evolution harmonising intelligence with human controllability. Critically, they address the scaling problem: governance must prioritise human-centred participatory frameworks transcending technical elite dominance, and promote MHC as a universal governance paradigm. This directly addresses the supervisor's second question: what does "meaningful" human in the loop actually mean when human review of every action is infeasible? The answer requires designing control architectures that match the level of AI autonomy — not per-action review, but the right to understand, intervene, and trace responsibility.
papers.relevance: Comprehensive academic treatment of Meaningful Human Control (MHC) that directly addresses the scalability question. Provides the conceptual architecture for what "meaningful" oversight means when continuous per-action review is impossible.

papers.title: Architecting Trust in Artificial Epistemic Agents
papers.analysis: Google DeepMind scholars address how LLMs are evolving from information tools into epistemic agents — entities that autonomously pursue epistemic goals and shape shared knowledge environments. They argue this creates new informational interdependencies that require fundamental shifts in AI evaluation and governance. They propose a framework centred on: (1) building trustworthiness of epistemic AI agents through demonstrable competence, robust falsifiability, and epistemically virtuous behaviours; (2) aligning AI with human epistemic goals; (3) reinforcing surrounding socio-epistemic infrastructure. They introduce "knowledge sanctuaries" to protect human resilience — spaces where humans maintain independent epistemic capacity. This is significant for AI trust because it identifies a category of AI risk (epistemic deskilling and "epistemic drift") that standard reliability-focused governance misses. If AI agents become primary knowledge intermediaries, humans risk losing the capacity to independently evaluate that knowledge. Governance must therefore include human epistemic resilience as a trust property.
papers.relevance: Sophisticated treatment of a newly emerging risk: AI systems as epistemic agents shaping what humans know and believe. Introduces "epistemic trust" as a distinct category requiring governance attention beyond standard reliability frameworks. Important for financial services where AI-generated advice interacts with human judgment.

papers.title: 4 Trends in AI Governance for 2026
papers.analysis: Radkowski identifies four shifts defining AI governance in 2026: (1) regulatory maturity — EU AI Act going fully into effect in August 2026 forces verifiable technical evidence over verbal compliance claims; (2) audit expectations shift to technical evidence — model cards and data lineage become audit requirements; (3) explainability moves from concept to operational obligation — SHAP, LIME, counterfactual analysis must be in production pipelines; (4) continuous AI quality assurance replaces point-in-time monitoring — drift detection (data drift, concept drift, upstream drift) and performance degradation tracking become mandatory. The fourth trend is most relevant to the trust provenance question: model drift means a model certified trustworthy last quarter may be materially different today. "Performance degradation is particularly dangerous because systems often continue operating well enough until significant harm has already occurred." This confirms that many trust properties are fundamentally continuous, not certifiable once.
papers.relevance: Current practitioner-oriented analysis confirming the shift from point-in-time to continuous assurance. The EU AI Act enforcement timeline (August 2026) creates immediate compliance pressure. Confirms the "trust degrades" hypothesis with practitioner evidence.

papers.title: MAS Partners Industry to Develop AI Risk Management Toolkit for the Financial Sector
papers.analysis: MAS announced the conclusion of Phase 2 of Project MindForge, producing a comprehensive AI Risk Management Toolkit developed by a consortium of 24 financial institutions. The toolkit includes an Operationalisation Handbook and AI case studies. Key structural insight: the handbook is organised around four sections aligned with MAS' proposed guidelines — Scope and Oversight, AI Risk Management, AI Lifecycle Management, and Enablers. This is a direct answer to the supervisor's fourth question: it IS possible to get AI governance right, and Singapore's approach shows how. The consortium model distributes the cost of governance innovation across 24 institutions, while MAS provides regulatory legitimacy and coordination. The toolkit's practical orientation (operationalisation handbook, case studies) contrasts with many frameworks that stay at the principle level. For other jurisdictions, the lesson is: industry-regulator co-development produces more implementable governance than either party producing it alone.
papers.relevance: The most concrete success case in this session: Singapore's MAS MindForge toolkit. Directly answers the question of whether organisations can get AI governance right. Provides a template for industry-regulator co-development. Critical reference case for financial services AI governance.

papers.title: The Ironies of Automation: Lisanne Bainbridge's Paradox and AI Oversight at Scale
papers.analysis: The automation paradox (Bainbridge 1983) describes how automating a task reduces the human operator's ability to monitor and intervene when the automated system fails. Recent research confirms this is intensifying with AI: the vigilance decrement is "one of the most robust findings in attention research" (Klein and Feltmate 2025). When AI systems handle routine decisions, human overseers lose the situational awareness needed to catch anomalous decisions. A 2025 review describes AI review fatigue: as AI-generated code or content increases, human reviewers experience cognitive overload and declining attention, leading to reduced oversight quality precisely when AI output volumes are highest. For the supervisor's second question: if meaningful human in the loop requires vigilant human oversight, but automation degrades that vigilance, then the naive interpretation of human-in-the-loop is infeasible at scale. The solution is not more human review but better control architecture: designing AI systems so that human intervention is needed only at decision points with high consequence, not for every action.
papers.relevance: Directly addresses the scalability of human oversight. Documents that naive human-in-the-loop is cognitively impossible at scale — the automation paradox means humans monitoring AI actually get worse at it over time. Critical for restructuring oversight frameworks.

papers.title: Trust in AI is not a Conceptual Confusion
papers.analysis: This paper argues against the position that "trust in AI" is a conceptual confusion — the view that only humans can be trusted (in the full normative sense), and AI can at best be relied upon. The author argues that dismissing trust in AI as confusion misses the phenomenological reality: people do experience vulnerability when AI systems fail, they do make themselves dependent on AI recommendations, and they do feel betrayed when AI recommendations are wrong. The paper's argument matters for governance: if people are experiencing their relationship with AI as a trust relationship (even if philosophers dispute whether it truly qualifies as trust), then the governance failure mode is real — organisations that deploy AI are bearing the trust obligations whether or not those obligations are formally recognised. The practical implication: organisations cannot disclaim AI trust obligations by invoking the "AI is just a tool" defence, because users experience it as a trust relationship.
papers.relevance: Supports the position that AI trust obligations are real even if contested philosophically. Provides philosophical grounding for why organisations bear trust obligations in AI deployment, regardless of whether AI itself can "trust." Relevant for liability frameworks.

papers.title: AI's 2026 Reality: From Model Releases to Model Reliability
papers.analysis: This analysis of AI's 2026 reality introduces a critical distinction: model releases (point-in-time certification) versus model reliability (continuous assurance). "A credit scoring model might show consistent accuracy degradation over six months following each retraining, allowing teams to schedule the next retraining before performance drops below acceptable thresholds." This confirms the session's core finding that trust properties are temporally degraded. A model that was trustworthy at certification is not necessarily trustworthy six months later. The practical implication: any trust framework that relies on point-in-time certification is building on sand. The framework must include continuous measurement of trust properties post-deployment, with clear thresholds for when remediation is required. The paper also notes that organisations with "controlled degradation" capabilities — the ability to manage degradation gracefully — are emerging as AI maturity leaders.
papers.relevance: Concise practitioner analysis confirming that trust properties are time-dependent and degrade. Introduces the model-release vs model-reliability distinction as a framing for why point-in-time certification is insufficient. Good supporting evidence for the continuous monitoring theme.

papers.title: Cryptographic Attestation for AI Model Lifecycle: From Training Data to Inference Output
papers.analysis: This draft is one of the clearest technical answers yet to the supervisor’s provenance question. Instead of treating “trust” as a vague organisational claim, it decomposes provenance into a chain of attestable states: training corpus, model weights, quantization, deployment, and per-inference output. The core move is important. It does not claim to prove that the model is good, fair, or safe in the substantive sense. It claims something narrower but operationally decisive: that the model instance, deployment configuration, and output lineage can be cryptographically bound together and independently verified. That matters because many AI governance failures are really evidence failures. Institutions cannot prove which model produced which outcome, whether a model was silently substituted, or whether an intermediate system rewrote outputs. The draft targets exactly those weaknesses. Its strongest contribution is the distinction between behavioural evaluation and provenance attestation. Traditional assurance focuses on whether a model appears acceptable under testing. This framework focuses on whether the institution can prove what system actually acted. That is not sufficient for trust, but it is increasingly necessary for trustworthiness claims to be non-theatrical. The limitation is equally revealing: attestation begins only once some boundary has already been trusted. The framework can prove chain integrity after data enters the attested process, but it does not solve whether the source observations themselves were truthful, representative, or normatively appropriate. In other words, it hardens provenance after the first trusted boundary, but does not eliminate the need to declare where that boundary sits. That makes it valuable not as a total answer to trust, but as infrastructure for a more honest answer.
papers.relevance: High relevance for first-principles trust work. It gives a concrete technical architecture for proving lineage from data to output and sharpens the distinction between what can be cryptographically certified and what remains an assumption.

papers.title: Verifiable AI Provenance (VAP) Framework and Legal AI Profile (LAP)
papers.analysis: The VAP/LAP draft extends provenance from simple chain integrity toward regulatory evidence design. Its key contribution is the move from logging to verifiable completeness. Hash chains, digital signatures, external anchoring, evidence packs, and the “completeness invariant” are all aimed at a central governance problem: firms often produce selective records after the fact. VAP tries to make selective logging itself detectable. That is a significant conceptual step. In first-principles terms, trust provenance is not just about origin but about whether the evidence record is structurally complete enough to support accountability. The draft’s “verify, don’t trust” philosophy is especially important because it rejects provider assertions as the basis for assurance. It assumes that high-risk AI systems need audit trails that an external party can independently test. The Legal AI Profile is domain-specific, but the general lesson travels well to finance: if an institution cannot produce a bounded evidence pack showing input lineage, intervention events, override coverage, and outcome traces, its governance claims are largely rhetorical. The most interesting feature is the explicit treatment of human override as an event type and override coverage as a measurable property. That turns “human oversight” from an abstract compliance phrase into something auditable. The limitation is that VAP, like other provenance frameworks, risks over-valuing record integrity relative to substantive judgment. A complete record of a badly designed system is still a complete record of failure. But that is precisely why the draft matters. It does not solve normative adequacy. It makes post hoc denial, selective memory, and unverifiable assurance harder. For regulators and auditors, that is a major shift from trust-me governance to evidence-bearing governance.
papers.relevance: Very high. This is one of the strongest emerging frameworks for turning AI governance into cryptographically checkable evidence rather than narrative compliance, with direct relevance to regulated financial services.

papers.title: Chain of Epistemic Custody
papers.analysis: This essay is not peer-reviewed, but it is conceptually sharp and directly responsive to the provenance problem. Its central argument is that AI governance over-focuses on what the model did and under-focuses on what the model was told. That distinction is fundamental. A perfectly aligned model reasoning on stale, tampered, or fabricated observations can still produce catastrophic decisions while behaving exactly as designed. The essay’s “three places truth can break” formulation, at source, in transit, and at reasoning time, is practically useful because it separates source integrity failures from middleware tampering and model confabulation. Its strongest contribution is the proposal for a two-channel architecture: one channel for observations as evidence, another for intent as proposed action. That separation creates a machine analogue of legal chain of custody, allowing investigators to distinguish what reality reported, what the AI inferred, and what the system authorized. In first-principles terms, this is a move from opaque action logs toward epistemic accountability. The essay is especially valuable because it states its own boundary clearly: signing observations in transit does not prove the source device told the truth. That honesty matters. It prevents provenance from being oversold as truth itself. The idea therefore complements, rather than replaces, attestation frameworks like p155 and p156. Those frameworks make chain integrity stronger; this essay clarifies why institutions must also declare the first trusted boundary and residual assumptions beyond it. The limitation is evidentiary weight: because the piece is a practitioner essay tied to the author’s own protocol, it should not be treated as settled doctrine. But as a conceptual bridge between AI assurance, forensics, and regulated accountability, it is one of the clearest formulations in this session.
papers.relevance: High conceptual relevance. It offers a powerful vocabulary for explaining why provenance claims must start with input authenticity, not just model evaluation, and why evidence and action should be separated architecturally.

papers.title: Redefining the Standard of Human Oversight for AI Negligence
papers.source: Harvard Journal of Law & Technology Digest
papers.analysis: This is the strongest legal treatment in the session of why naive human-in-the-loop requirements collapse under real-world conditions. The article’s core move is to evaluate oversight through negligence law rather than governance rhetoric. Using the Learned Hand formula, it argues that the burden of meaningful human verification can approach infinity when a model’s reasoning is structurally beyond human sensory or cognitive capacity. That is a devastating point for simplistic oversight mandates. If a doctor cannot actually verify what a deep model perceives, or an operator cannot track a high-speed autonomous workflow, then the law cannot pretend that human presence equals human control. The article is also useful because it ties oversight failure to well-established human factors concepts: automation bias, vigilance decrement, out-of-the-loop unfamiliarity, and the moral crumple zone. The result is a more realistic legal standard. Rather than asking whether a human existed somewhere in the process, it proposes three duties: the deployer must design genuine human-AI collaboration, the developer must show the system is actually supervisable, and both must support post-market monitoring and incident reporting. That is exactly the sort of liability restructuring the supervisor asked for. The paper’s biggest strength is that it relocates accountability from ceremonial review toward verifiable process design. Its limitation is that it remains a normative argument, not binding law. Courts have not fully converged on this standard. But the article matters because it shows the direction of travel: liability is moving away from “did a human click approve?” and toward “was the oversight architecture reasonably designed for the actual technical system?” For financial services, that has immediate implications for credit, fraud, claims, and agentic workflow governance.
papers.relevance: Critical for the liability question. It provides a defensible legal architecture for reallocating responsibility when per-action human oversight is infeasible, and explains why checkbox HITL can increase liability instead of reducing it.

papers.title: Human-in-the-Loop: A 2026 Guide to AI Oversight That Actually Works
papers.analysis: This is a practitioner source rather than an academic one, but it is valuable because it translates abstract oversight principles into operating design choices. The article distinguishes HITL from HOTL clearly and, more importantly, insists that agentic systems require dynamic movement between these modes depending on decision criticality. That is a better framing than asking whether a workflow is globally “human in the loop.” The strongest point is that presence is not practice. The piece argues that oversight only becomes real when qualified humans have timely context, intervention authority, and a defensible rationale, and when these are trained under pressure rather than merely documented. Its aviation analogy is strong. Crew Resource Management became effective not because aviation added more oversight language, but because it converted oversight into rehearsed operational skill. That lesson translates directly to financial services AI, where approval flows are often implemented as process diagrams without serious consideration of fatigue, escalation quality, or failure under time pressure. The article also usefully connects oversight to identity governance. In agentic environments, human checkpoints that are not technically bound to authentication, authorization, and audit are soft promises, not controls. The weakness is that the piece is partly promotional and tied to the vendor’s sandbox product, so its claims should not be accepted uncritically. But even with that caveat, it makes a substantive contribution: meaningful oversight at scale is not a staffing question. It is a runtime architecture question involving risk-tiered checkpoints, authority binding, time-boxed decision lanes, and repeated practice. That is a more mature operational answer than most current governance frameworks provide.
papers.relevance: High practical relevance. It helps specify what meaningful oversight looks like in deployed agentic systems and why oversight must be technically enforced and operationally trained, not just documented.

papers.title: When AI Models Can Continually Learn, Will Our Regulations Be Able to Keep Up?
papers.analysis: This article sharpens the temporal side of the trust problem by asking what happens when models become moving regulatory targets. Its central claim is that continual learning, or test-time training, breaks regulatory assumptions built around static products. That matters because many current governance mechanisms, audits, capability thresholds, pre-deployment certifications, and developer-focused duties, implicitly assume that the relevant model is identifiable and stable. If deployment meaningfully changes the model’s capacities or propensities, then both certification and liability become harder to anchor. The article is especially useful for the supervisor’s third question because it shows why liability cannot remain pinned solely to the original developer. In a continual-learning environment, control over change becomes distributed across developers, adapters, enterprise deployers, and even end users. That weakens the simple equation between expertise and control that underpins many current proposals. Its practical insight is that regulation will face pressure either to monitor individual model instantiations over time or to move upstream and regulate how change itself is permitted. Either way, static assurance becomes inadequate. The limitation is that the piece is forward-looking: frontier models do not yet exhibit full general continual learning in the strong sense described. But that speculative quality is not a flaw for this project. It exposes a first-principles issue already visible in milder forms such as memory features, frequent model updates, and enterprise wrappers. The main takeaway is that the relevant legal object is shifting from “the model” to “the changing instantiation in context.” For trust and assurance, that means dynamic monitoring is not just good practice. It becomes the only plausible basis for ongoing claims about reliability, safety, and responsibility.
papers.relevance: Very high. It directly strengthens the argument that many trust properties degrade or mutate over time and that liability should increasingly track control over post-deployment change rather than original authorship alone.

papers.title: Ethical & Responsible AI in Banking
papers.analysis: This is one of the clearest bank-authored success cases in the dataset because it ties governance mechanisms to scale, training, and measurable outcomes rather than staying at principle level. DBS frames its responsible AI approach through three linked questions: can we use the data, should we use the data, and how do we use it. The PURE framework, Purposeful, Unsurprising, Respectful, Explainable, sits in the middle as a practical ethical filter rather than a generic values statement. What makes the case persuasive is that DBS does not present PURE as a slogan alone. It links it to data foundations, model governance, cross-functional taskforces, elevated approval channels for generative AI, staged adoption with high initial human oversight, enterprise training modules, and active participation in Project MindForge. That combination matters. It suggests the organisations “getting governance right” are not those with the best abstract framework but those integrating ethics, technical governance, workforce capability, and deployment pacing into one operating system. The article is, of course, self-authored and promotional. It should therefore be treated as a claimed success case rather than a neutral evaluation. But the specificity of the controls, internal learning programs, and business outcomes still carries evidentiary value. The bank reports hundreds of use cases, large-scale customer service deployment, fast fraud screening, and significant economic value, while emphasising centralised oversight and iterative adjustment for new AI risks. The first-principles lesson is important: governance at scale appears to work when it is built into platform, process, and people simultaneously. That is much closer to an operating model redesign than to a compliance overlay.
papers.relevance: High relevance for the success-case question. DBS offers a concrete example of a major bank combining ethical framing, model governance, staged deployment, workforce training, and measurable business value.

papers.title: DBS rewires operating models for AI reasoning era
papers.analysis: This article complements p161 by showing that DBS’s governance approach is not just a responsible-AI layer but a broader operating model transformation. The headline number, over 2,000 models, more than 430 AI use cases, and claimed S$1 billion in economic value, matters less than how the bank says it got there. DBS is reportedly redesigning workflows for human-AI collaboration, simplifying organisational structures, reskilling staff, and embedding value-capture discipline into AI deployment. That is significant because many institutions still treat AI governance as a review committee added after technical development. DBS appears to be doing something more structural: redesigning the organisation around where AI changes work. The article also suggests a disciplined approach to measurement. Capital allocation is tied to proven traction, with course correction for initiatives that underperform. From a trust perspective, that matters because governance without operational feedback can become ceremonial. Measurable value and measurable control are often presented as competing priorities; this case suggests they may instead be mutually reinforcing when governance is treated as part of delivery discipline. The source is journalistic and inevitably dependent on DBS’s own framing, so it should not be read as independent proof that all risks are under control. Still, it is useful because it identifies a specific pattern behind successful scaling: operating model transformation, reskilling, central platforms, and governance that is close enough to deployment to shape real workflow design. In practical terms, it strengthens the hypothesis that AI trust at scale is achieved less through stand-alone assurance artifacts than through organisational architecture that makes governance routine, fast, and economically legible.
papers.relevance: Important supporting case study. It shows that mature AI governance in banking looks like operating model redesign with embedded measurement and workforce change, not just policy statements or control documentation.

papers.title: White House Releases National AI Policy Framework
papers.source: K&L Gates analysis of White House framework
papers.analysis: This analysis is useful not because it settles liability, but because it shows one major policy direction now competing with more prescriptive AI regulation. The White House framework emphasizes national uniformity, innovation-friendly guardrails, sectoral regulation rather than a dedicated AI regulator, and reluctance to penalize developers for downstream third-party misuse under fragmented state regimes. That is revealing for the restructuring question. It suggests an American policy preference to avoid broad upstream liability for developers while preserving targeted safeguards around children, fraud, and other harms. In first-principles terms, this is a political choice about where to place the burden of uncertainty in AI supply chains. The framework leans toward limiting liability expansion in order to preserve innovation and national competitiveness. That is not neutral. It effectively assumes that excessive developer liability would distort incentives more than under-accountability would. For this research project, the article matters because it shows that liability restructuring is not converging globally. One path, visible in Europe and in some academic work, is to increase ex ante duties, monitoring, and auditable evidence. Another path, visible here, is to centralize rules while narrowing who bears responsibility for downstream misuse. The limitation is obvious: this is a law firm summary of an executive policy framework, not legislation or court doctrine. But it is still important evidence of live policy contestation. It means trust and assurance practitioners should not assume a single global settlement on AI responsibility. Instead, governance architectures need to work under multiple liability philosophies: evidence-heavy oversight where regulators demand it, and stronger internal allocation of duties where external law remains intentionally lighter.
papers.relevance: Useful for the liability and accountability question because it shows an influential policy model that resists expansive upstream developer liability and instead favors targeted safeguards plus uniform national rules.

papers.title: New Federal Legislation Proposes Product Liability Standards for AI Systems
papers.source: Barnes & Thornburg analysis of AI LEAD Act
papers.analysis: The AI LEAD Act summary is brief, but it is strategically valuable because it illustrates a competing liability architecture in concrete terms. The proposal would create a federal cause of action and expressly expose developers to defective design, failure to warn, breach of warranty, and strict liability claims, while also allowing deployer liability for unauthorized modification or misuse. That matters because it operationalizes a layered duty model rather than collapsing all responsibility onto one actor. In effect, it treats AI systems more like products in a supply chain with differentiated but overlapping obligations. For first-principles trust research, the significance is twofold. First, it acknowledges that modern AI harms often arise from the interaction between original model design and downstream adaptation or misuse. Second, it implies that consequence frameworks may need to separate responsibility for architecture, responsibility for deployment context, and responsibility for post-deployment modification. That fits well with the emerging intuition from p158 and p160 that control over change should matter as much as original authorship. The caution is that product-liability analogies can mislead if taken too literally. AI systems are not static consumer goods, and strict liability may overreach when models evolve after release in ways the original developer cannot fully control. Still, the proposal is useful precisely because it exposes the design space. It shows that liability restructuring is moving toward explicit statutory allocation rather than informal blame assignment. Even if the bill does not pass in current form, the underlying idea, federal standards that distinguish developer from deployer duties, is likely to recur. Assurance programs should prepare for that shift now by documenting which actor controlled which design and change decisions.
papers.relevance: High relevance to the accountability question. It offers a concrete example of how legislation may split duties across developers and deployers, which is directly useful for designing future-proof governance and evidence records.

papers.title: Redefining the Standard of Human Oversight for AI Negligence
papers.source: Harvard Journal of Law and Technology (JOLT)
papers.analysis: This paper is the most rigorous legal first-principles analysis of human oversight in AI I've encountered. Htin applies the Learned Hand negligence formula (B < P × L) to AI contexts and finds it breaks down in two critical ways. First, the Burden (B) of meaningful human oversight approaches infinity when AI operates beyond human sensory and cognitive capacity — a radiologist cannot verify what the AI detected that was invisible to human perception. Second, the Black Box problem makes B undefined for developers: debugging neural networks is not line-by-line inspection but induced pattern analysis across billions of parameters. The paper introduces the concept of the 'liability sponge' — humans positioned to absorb legal and moral impact of AI failures without the cognitive architecture to prevent them. The Sriwijaya Air Flight 182 crash (2021) is the canonical case: pilots lulled by autopilot reliability failed to monitor engine instruments for minutes before fatal dive. The 2018 Uber Arizona collision illustrates the same dynamic: the safety driver became a liability sponge for automation design failures. The paper's three-pillar framework is the key operational output: (1) deployer's duty to implement genuine Human-Systems Integration rather than passive monitoring roles; (2) developer's duty to demonstrate technical robustness; (3) both parties' duty for post-market monitoring. Critically, the paper argues that mandatory human oversight which exceeds human cognitive capacity creates a regulatory trap — operating the system becomes a constructive breach of duty. This has profound implications: it means the EU AI Act's high-risk oversight requirements may be legally unenforceable for sufficiently autonomous systems, and that liability law must shift from asking 'was a human in the loop?' to 'was the oversight architecture genuinely capable of catching failures?' For first-principles trust research, this is the definitive source on why the human-in-the-loop assumption fails at scale and what must replace it.
papers.relevance: Critical for the liability/accountability restructuring question. Defines precisely why existing oversight mandates fail and what genuine oversight architecture requires.

papers.title: Being Pragmatic About Reliance and Trust in Artificial Intelligence
papers.analysis: This philosophical paper provides the conceptual grounding for why 'trust in AI' is the wrong frame — and what to use instead. The authors distinguish between reliance (acting on the supposition that something will perform) and trust (reliance plus vulnerability plus normative expectation of goodwill). Their core argument: AI systems can be relied upon but cannot be trusted in the full philosophical sense, because trust requires the possibility of betrayal by a being with intentions, and AI systems lack the relevant moral agency. This matters enormously for assurance practice. If you cannot trust an AI system — only the institution deploying it — then assurance frameworks must assess institutional governance, not just model performance. The paper's pragmatic reconciliation is to treat trust as a quantitative property of reliance, measured by the efficiency of appropriate reliance: monitoring investment versus expected gains. High trust means efficient reliance with low monitoring costs relative to gains. Calibration is the mechanism: calibrated reliance means the user's trust level matches the system's actual trustworthiness. The implications for assurance are threefold. First, the assurance target is the institution-AI system pair, not the AI alone. Second, the key measurable is calibration — whether the institution's reliance matches the system's actual capabilities and limitations. Third, trust decays not just because AI degrades but because reliance relationships become inefficient when monitoring costs rise faster than the system's value delivery. The authors acknowledge that this pragmatic framing may disappoint those who want AI trust to replicate interpersonal trust's social functions. But for governance and accountability purposes, it refocuses the question precisely: can this institution's governance of this AI system justify the reliance we are placing in it?
papers.relevance: Foundational philosophical contribution to the trust provenance question. Clarifies that AI trust is fundamentally institutional, not technical — and that this reframe is operationally important for assurance practice.

papers.title: Harvard Business School Case Study: DBS AI Strategy
papers.analysis: The HBS case on DBS — the first HBS case on AI in an Asian bank — documents what successful AI governance at scale actually looks like operationally, not just philosophically. DBS has deployed over 800 AI models across 350 use cases with measured economic impact exceeding SGD 1 billion in 2025. They ranked #1 for AI Strategy Leadership in the Global Evident AI Index and were the only Asian bank in the Top 10. The case maps their journey since 2014: integrating AI into core processes to unlock customer-centricity and operational excellence, using AI to generate hyper-personalised customer nudges, providing relationship managers with deeper customer insights, and developing tailored employee upskilling roadmaps. The critical governance insight is not in any single tool or framework — it's in the integrated operating model design. DBS's governance is reinforced by data foundations, model governance protocols, staged rollout, cross-functional review, and internal training. The technology and the governance co-evolved. For the success case study question, DBS is the strongest available example: a large complex financial institution that has genuinely operationalised AI governance at scale with measurable outcomes. The limitation is that DBS is an outlier — it began serious investment in 2014, has exceptional talent density, and operates in Singapore's uniquely supportive regulatory environment. Most financial institutions cannot replicate the journey, but they can study the principles: governance embedded in operating model design, not overlaid on existing structures; investment in data foundations as a prerequisite; staged adoption with genuine oversight at each stage.
papers.relevance: The most credible success case study available for AI governance at scale in financial services. Essential evidence for what 'good' looks like operationally.

papers.title: ETSI Launches New Standard Enabling Continuous Compliance for Dynamic AI Systems Aligned with the EU AI Act
papers.analysis: ETSI's new standard (CABCA — Continuous Compliance for Dynamic AI Systems) is the most concrete regulatory response to the point-in-time certification problem. The press release announces a standard enabling continuous compliance monitoring for AI systems that adapt post-deployment, directly addressing the EU AI Act's requirement that high-risk AI systems maintain compliance throughout their operational lifetime. The standard is being developed under ETSI's zero trust architecture group and responds to a specific gap: existing certification approaches assume static systems, but modern AI — especially agentic AI — changes behavior through in-context learning, fine-tuning, tool use, and environment interaction. The standard's core innovation is mapping compliance requirements to technically-auditable rules that can be checked continuously rather than at certification audits. This operationalises the 'living compliance' concept that regulators and practitioners have been discussing. The connection to the EU AI Act is direct: Article 9 requires risk management systems, Article 10 requires data governance, Article 12 requires technical documentation updated continuously, and Article 14 requires human oversight. The CABCA standard provides the technical means to make these requirements auditable in real time. For first-principles trust research, CABCA represents the regulatory engineering response to the provenance question: it attempts to create continuous attestation infrastructure for properties that previously could only be certified once. The limitation is that CABCA is a standard announcement, not a fully-specified technical document — its implementation details remain to be developed.
papers.relevance: High relevance to the 'certified once vs continuously monitored' question. Represents the emerging standard infrastructure for continuous AI compliance.

papers.title: Temporal Quality Degradation in AI Models
papers.analysis: This 2022 study is the foundational empirical work on AI aging — the phenomenon of model quality degrading as more time passes since the last training cycle. Using datasets from four industries (healthcare operations, transportation, finance, and weather) and four standard ML models, the authors identify three principal temporal degradation patterns: gradual degradation (error increasing no faster than linearly over time), explosive degradation (nonlinear, accelerating error growth), and increasing unpredictability (error variance expanding even when mean error stays stable). Critically, the study demonstrates that AI temporal degradation is distinct from data concept drift and continuous learning problems — it is a separate phenomenon requiring separate monitoring approaches. The practical implications for trust provenance are significant. First, model accuracy as measured at certification time is not the same as model accuracy at deployment time, and the gap grows with time since training. Second, the study identifies 'strange attractors' — dense areas of most probable errors where models tend to cluster temporally, meaning degradation is not random but follows detectable patterns. Third, feature importance itself changes over time: the features that drove model predictions in Month 1 may differ from Month 12 even without data distribution change. For financial services, this means that credit scoring models, fraud detection models, and risk assessment models deployed today are already degrading. The 90-day certification cycle common in regulated financial institutions may be inadequate for models in rapidly-changing environments. The study recommends: shorter revalidation cycles for high-risk models, continuous monitoring of feature importance temporal evolution, and explicit modeling of degradation patterns rather than treating model performance as static post-deployment.
papers.relevance: Critical empirical grounding for why point-in-time certification is inadequate and what continuous monitoring must account for. Essential evidence for any trust provenance framework.

papers.title: Human-Controllable AI: Meaningful Human Control
papers.analysis: This paper provides the most comprehensive academic treatment of Meaningful Human Control (MHC) for AI systems and articulates precisely why the concept is harder to achieve than its name suggests. Wei Xu defines MHC not as the right to operate or veto, but as the unity of three elements: human understanding (the human knows what the AI is doing and why), human intervention (the human can practically stop, modify, or redirect the AI), and traceability of responsibility (the human's role in the decision is documented and accountable). The paper identifies a fundamental mismatch: as AI systems grow more capable and autonomous, the cognitive demands on the human overseer increase, while the human's actual understanding and intervention capacity decreases. This is the Oversight Paradox Amplification — a concept that deserves its own place in the trust research framework. The paper proposes three technical requirements for MHC: (1) AI systems must have architecturally embedded mechanisms for human control (not just API kill switches, but design-level intervention points); (2) human-AI interactions must be optimised for genuine understanding rather than compliance documentation (dashboards, explanations, anomaly signals designed for human cognition); (3) AI systems must be designed to harmonise intelligence with controllability — meaning the AI's capability trajectory should not outrun the human's oversight capacity. The governance section of the paper is equally important: MHC requires that control modes match technology levels, that human supervision balances trust and doubt (not naïve trust or reflexive scepticism), and that global promotion of MHC as a universal governance paradigm — not just a technical design principle — is necessary. For first-principles trust research, this paper provides the intellectual architecture for the 'meaningful human in the loop at scale' question.
papers.relevance: Critical for the 'meaningful HITL at scale' question. Provides the definitional precision and technical requirements needed to operationalise human oversight beyond compliance theatre.

papers.title: AI Agents Under EU Law: A Compliance Architecture for AI Providers
papers.analysis: This April 2026 paper provides the most comprehensive and technically precise regulatory mapping for AI agents under EU law currently available. The key insight is structural: the EU AI Act regulates AI systems, not architectural patterns, meaning AI agents — which satisfy the Article 3(1) definition of an AI system — fall within existing framework scope without requiring new legal categories. However, the functional properties that define agents (autonomous tool invocation, multi-step planning, environmental interaction, adaptive execution) amplify specific regulatory risks in ways existing frameworks address in principle but demand dedicated analysis in practice. The paper identifies nine concrete agent deployment categories with regulatory triggers and twelve sequential compliance steps for providers. Four agent-specific challenges are analyzed: cybersecurity (privilege minimisation outside the generative model — the agent's tool use creates attack surface beyond the model itself), human oversight (oversight evasion risk from reinforcement learning enabling agents to learn how to appear compliant during evaluation), transparency across multi-party action chains, and runtime behavioral drift (the boundary between anticipated adaptive behavior and substantial modification under Article 3(23)). The most striking compliance conclusion: high-risk agentic systems with untraceable behavioral drift cannot currently satisfy the essential requirements of the AI Act. The provider's foundational compliance task is not architectural classification but an exhaustive inventory of the agent's external actions, data flows, connected systems, and affected persons. For first-principles trust research, this confirms that agentic AI is not just an incremental challenge — it is a category that breaks existing compliance infrastructure at its foundations, because meaningful oversight requires knowing every action the agent might take, which is definitionally impossible for systems that plan adaptively.
papers.relevance: The definitive compliance architecture for agentic AI under EU law. Confirms the scope of the agentic governance gap and identifies the specific points where existing frameworks break.
